text: What is a database?
options: A specialized type of programming language
An organized collection of structured data
A specific type of network protocol used
A physical hardware component in a system
explanation: A database is an organized collection of structured data stored electronically.

text: Which of the following is a popular Database Management System (DBMS)?
options: CSS
HTML
MySQL
FTP
explanation: MySQL is a widely used relational database management system.

text: What does DBMS stand for?
options: Data Backup Management System
Database Management System
Database Modification Software
Digital Base Management System
explanation: DBMS stands for Database Management System.

text: Which of these is an advantage of using a database system?
options: Data cannot be shared between users
Data redundancy is significantly reduced
Overall storage cost increases greatly
Programs become much harder to write
explanation: Database systems reduce data redundancy by centralizing data storage.

text: What is data redundancy?
options: Algorithmic compression of data
Unnecessary duplication of data
Permanent loss of stored data
Cryptographic encryption of data
explanation: Data redundancy refers to unnecessary duplication of data in a system.

text: Who manages and controls access to a database?
options: Business Systems Analyst
Database Administrator (DBA)
Network Systems Engineer
Web Interface Designer
explanation: The DBA is responsible for managing and controlling access to the database.

text: Which of the following is NOT a type of database user?
options: DBA administrator
Database end user
SQL programmer
Compiler program
explanation: A compiler is not a type of database user; it is a software tool.

text: What is the primary goal of a DBMS?
options: To manage and configure physical hardware components in a server rack
To provide a convenient and efficient way to store and retrieve data
To design and develop interactive websites and web applications online
To replace the functionality of the underlying operating system entirely
explanation: The primary goal of DBMS is to provide convenient and efficient data storage and retrieval.

text: In a file-based system, data is stored in:
options: Random access memory storage only
Separate files for each application
Cloud-based storage systems only
A single centralized data repository
explanation: In file-based systems, each application maintains its own separate data files.

text: Data independence means:
options: Changes to data storage do not affect application programs
Applications and data definitions are treated as the same
Stored data values cannot be modified by any user at all
Data items are not stored permanently on the storage disk
explanation: Data independence means application programs are insulated from changes in data storage.

text: Which level of data abstraction describes how data is actually stored?
options: View level
Logical level
External level
Physical level
explanation: The physical level describes how data is actually stored on storage media.

text: The three-schema architecture was proposed by:
options: IEEE standards body
ISO organization
W3C consortium
ANSI/SPARC committee
explanation: The three-schema architecture was proposed by the ANSI/SPARC committee.

text: Which of the following is a disadvantage of DBMS?
options: Increased data redundancy across tables
Program-data dependence across modules
High initial cost of hardware and software
Data inconsistency between applications
explanation: DBMS requires significant initial investment in hardware, software, and training.

text: What is metadata in a database context?
options: Temporary data
Data about data
Deleted data
Encrypted data
explanation: Metadata is data that describes the structure and properties of other data.

text: Which component of DBMS is responsible for ensuring data consistency?
options: Integrity manager
Authorization manager
Buffer pool manager
File system manager
explanation: The integrity manager ensures that data satisfies integrity constraints.

text: A data dictionary stores:
options: Application programs and their code
Actual user data stored in tables
Metadata about the database structure
Backup files created periodically
explanation: A data dictionary stores metadata such as schema definitions and constraints.

text: Which of the following describes logical data independence?
options: Ability to change applications without affecting the underlying data
Ability to change the logical schema without affecting external schemas
None of these options correctly describes logical data independence
Ability to change physical storage without affecting the logical schema
explanation: Logical data independence is the ability to change the logical schema without affecting external schemas.

text: What is a database instance?
options: The database management software application
The overall architectural design of the database
A backup copy of the database stored on disk
The collection of data stored at a particular moment
explanation: A database instance is the snapshot of data stored in a database at a particular moment.

text: A database schema is:
options: A data structure used for indexing records
A language used for querying the database
The overall design or structure of the database
The actual data values stored in the tables
explanation: A schema defines the logical structure and organization of a database.

text: Which of the following is NOT a function of a DBMS?
options: Generating source code for applications
Manipulating and querying stored data
Managing concurrent transaction access
Defining and managing data structures
explanation: Generating application source code is not a DBMS function.

text: In the ANSI/SPARC three-schema architecture, which mapping exists between the conceptual and internal schemas?
options: Application/Internal mapping
External/Conceptual mapping
Conceptual/Internal mapping
External/Internal mapping
explanation: The conceptual/internal mapping defines correspondence between conceptual and internal schemas.

text: Which of the following best describes the impedance mismatch problem?
options: Differences between database model and programming language data types
Mismatch between primary key constraints and foreign key references
Slow network connectivity between the client and the database server
Hardware incompatibility between different types of storage devices
explanation: Impedance mismatch refers to the differences between database models and programming language constructs.

text: Self-describing nature of a database system means:
options: The system contains metadata along with the data itself
Users do not need to interact with it in any way
The database automatically generates its own queries
Data stored in the system is automatically encrypted
explanation: Self-describing means the database catalog stores descriptions of the database structure (metadata) along with the data.

text: Program-data independence is primarily achieved through:
options: Avoiding the use of all index structures
Using only one single programming language
Data abstraction and catalog separation
Upgrading to faster processing hardware
explanation: By separating program definitions from data definitions and storing metadata in the catalog, program-data independence is achieved.

text: Which of the following is a consequence of violating data independence?
options: Concurrent transaction handling is improved across the system
Data security and access controls are substantially improved
The system achieves significantly faster query processing speed
Application programs must be modified when data storage changes
explanation: Without data independence, changes in storage require costly application modifications.

text: The database approach provides controlled redundancy by:
options: Encrypting all data stored in the system
Avoiding the use of keys or constraints
Integrating data and applying normalization
Storing data in multiple separate files
explanation: Controlled redundancy is achieved by integrating data into a central repository and applying normalization techniques.

text: Which type of DBMS supports complex data types like spatial and multimedia data?
options: Flat file based DBMS
Network model DBMS
Hierarchical tree DBMS
Object-Relational DBMS
explanation: Object-Relational DBMS extends relational systems to support complex data types.

text: In a multi-user DBMS environment, the concurrency control subsystem is essential to:
options: Perform scheduled database backup operations to external storage
Ensure correct results when multiple users access data simultaneously
Speed up the processing of single-user queries on a local database
Manage the allocation of available disk space across the system
explanation: Concurrency control ensures data integrity when multiple transactions execute concurrently.

text: Which of the following database utilities is used to reorganize data for better performance?
options: Statistical analysis utility
Backup restore utility
Reorganization utility
Data loading utility
explanation: The reorganization utility restructures the database files for improved performance.

text: Data abstraction in DBMS hides complexity at different levels. Which sequence is correct from lowest to highest?
options: External → Conceptual → Internal
Conceptual → Internal → External
External → Internal → Conceptual
Internal → Conceptual → External
explanation: The correct order from lowest to highest abstraction is Internal (physical), Conceptual (logical), External (view).

text: The two-tier architecture in databases consists of:
options: Three layer tiers
Client and Server
Only Client tier
Only Server tier
explanation: Two-tier architecture has a client tier and a server tier.

text: In a client-server architecture, the database resides on the:
options: Client side
Neither side
Both equally
Server side
explanation: In client-server architecture, the database resides on the server.

text: What is a centralized database system?
options: A system designed to operate without any DBMS software layer
A system that runs exclusively on cloud computing platforms
A system with data distributed across multiple remote locations
A system where data is stored and managed at a single location
explanation: A centralized database system stores and manages all data at a single site.

text: Which tier in three-tier architecture handles the user interface?
options: Presentation tier
Database data tier
None of these tiers
Business logic tier
explanation: The presentation tier handles the user interface in three-tier architecture.

text: What does a query processor do?
options: Backs up data to external storage
Interprets and executes database queries
Stores data on physical disk drives
Manages user accounts and profiles
explanation: The query processor interprets, optimizes, and executes database queries.

text: The storage manager in a DBMS is responsible for:
options: Creating graphical user interfaces
Managing how data is stored on disk
Writing and executing SQL queries
Configuring network communication
explanation: The storage manager handles the interaction between the DBMS and the physical storage.

text: Which component of DBMS translates high-level queries into low-level operations?
options: Transaction manager
Query compiler module
Buffer manager module
File manager module
explanation: The query compiler translates high-level queries like SQL into low-level file operations.

text: In a three-tier architecture, the middle tier is called:
options: Data warehouse store
Application server
Database server
Web browser client
explanation: The middle tier is the application server that contains business logic.

text: What is the role of the buffer manager?
options: Creates and maintains database schemas and table structures
Manages the transfer of data between disk and main memory
Manages user authentication and role-based access control
Processes and optimizes SQL queries for efficient execution
explanation: The buffer manager handles reading and writing data blocks between disk and memory.

text: A database engine is also known as:
options: Query language
Storage engine
Front-end application
Report generator
explanation: The database engine (storage engine) handles data storage, retrieval, and update.

text: In the three-schema architecture, which schema defines the user's view?
options: External schema
Internal schema
Conceptual schema
Physical schema
explanation: The external schema defines individual user views of the database.

text: Which DBMS component ensures that the database remains in a consistent state after a failure?
options: Authorization manager
Buffer manager
Recovery manager
Query optimizer
explanation: The recovery manager restores the database to a consistent state after failures.

text: The data definition language (DDL) compiler processes:
options: Application programs written in the host language
Schema definitions and stores metadata in the catalog
User-submitted queries for data retrieval operations
Data insertion commands for populating new tables
explanation: The DDL compiler processes schema definitions and stores them as metadata in the system catalog.

text: In a two-tier architecture, the application logic resides:
options: Exclusively on the client tier
Exclusively on the server tier
On a separate dedicated middle tier
On the client side or server side
explanation: In two-tier architecture, application logic can reside on either the client or the server.

text: What is a database catalog?
options: A system database containing metadata about the database structure
A comprehensive list of all users who have database access rights
An index structure built on primary keys of all related tables
A sequential log file that records all committed transactions
explanation: The database catalog (system catalog) stores metadata including schema definitions and constraints.

text: Which architecture is most suitable for web-based database applications?
options: Peer-to-peer architecture
Two-tier architecture
Single-tier architecture
Three-tier architecture
explanation: Three-tier architecture (client, application server, database server) is most suitable for web applications.

text: The runtime database processor executes:
options: Backup and recovery operations only
Data definition language statements only
Schema compilation processes only
Privileged commands and query plans at runtime
explanation: The runtime database processor executes database access operations at runtime.

text: What is the purpose of the DML precompiler?
options: To compile DDL commands and update the system catalog
To extract DML commands embedded in application programs
To manage concurrency control among active transactions
To optimize queries by selecting the best execution plan
explanation: The DML precompiler extracts DML commands from application programs for processing.

text: In a parallel database system, the main goal is:
options: Reduced overall storage cost on disk
Simplified query writing for users
Eliminating the need for any backups
Improved performance through parallelism
explanation: Parallel database systems use multiple processors to improve query processing performance.

text: Shared-nothing architecture means:
options: All processors share the memory and disk
Only disk is shared among all processors
Each processor has its own memory and disk
Only memory is shared among processors
explanation: In shared-nothing architecture, each processor has its own private memory and disk.

text: In a shared-disk parallel architecture, the potential bottleneck is:
options: The interconnection network to the shared disk
The CPU processing speed of each processor
Query parsing and syntax validation steps
Memory allocation among running transactions
explanation: Shared-disk architecture can bottleneck at the interconnection network since all processors access the same disk.

text: The query optimizer in a DBMS uses which approach to find the best execution plan?
options: Cost-based optimization
Random selection method
User preference settings
Alphabetical table ordering
explanation: Cost-based optimization evaluates multiple execution plans and selects the one with the lowest estimated cost.

text: Which type of parallelism involves executing different operations of a query simultaneously?
options: Inter-query parallelism
None of these types
Intra-query parallelism
Pipeline parallelism
explanation: Inter-query parallelism executes different queries simultaneously, while intra-query parallelism splits a single query.

text: In a DBMS architecture, the catalog manager is responsible for:
options: Managing user connections and authentication sessions
Handling network protocols and data transmission tasks
Processing application logic and business rule validation
Storing and retrieving schema information and statistics
explanation: The catalog manager maintains metadata, schema information, and statistics used by the query optimizer.

text: The concept of data independence is directly supported by:
options: Using only a single schema without any additional views
Storing all data exclusively in flat file format on disk
Avoiding the use of views or external schema definitions
The three-level architecture with mappings between levels
explanation: Data independence is supported by the three-level architecture where mappings between levels absorb changes.

text: In a DBMS, the lock manager is part of which subsystem?
options: Storage management subsystem
Communication layer subsystem
Concurrency control subsystem
Query processing subsystem
explanation: The lock manager belongs to the concurrency control subsystem, managing locks for concurrent access.

text: What is the main advantage of shared-memory parallel architecture?
options: Lower hardware costs compared to other parallel architectures
Fast communication between processors through shared memory
No synchronization needed between the concurrent processors
No need for an operating system to coordinate the processes
explanation: Shared-memory architecture enables fast inter-processor communication through shared memory.

text: The DBMS buffer replacement strategy LRU stands for:
options: Least Recently Used
Last Record Updated
Latest Read Utility
Least Required Update
explanation: LRU (Least Recently Used) is a buffer replacement strategy that evicts the least recently accessed page.

text: Which DBMS component is responsible for enforcing authorization and integrity constraints?
options: Network manager and router module
Query parser and syntax analyzer
Storage manager and file handler
Authorization and integrity manager
explanation: The authorization and integrity manager checks permissions and ensures data satisfies integrity constraints.

text: In a multi-tier architecture, which tier contains the business rules?
options: Data tier (bottom tier)
Application tier (middle tier)
Client tier (front tier)
Presentation tier (top tier)
explanation: Business rules and application logic reside in the application tier (middle tier).

text: Which data model organizes data in tables of rows and columns?
options: Relational model
Object model
Hierarchical model
Network model
explanation: The relational model organizes data into tables (relations) consisting of rows and columns.

text: In the hierarchical data model, data is organized as a:
options: Graph structure
Star structure
Tree structure
Table structure
explanation: The hierarchical model organizes data in a tree-like parent-child structure.

text: An Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram is used to:
options: Design network topologies for servers
Create application interfaces for users
Model the conceptual design of a database
Write SQL queries against stored tables
explanation: ER diagrams are used for conceptual database design showing entities and their relationships.

text: In an ER diagram, a rectangle represents:
options: An entity type
A relationship
An attribute
A primary key
explanation: Rectangles represent entities in an ER diagram.

text: An attribute in a data model represents:
options: A constraint on records
A property of an entity
A table in the database
A relationship between entities
explanation: An attribute represents a property or characteristic of an entity.

text: Which symbol represents a relationship in an ER diagram?
options: Ellipse
Rectangle
Triangle
Diamond
explanation: A diamond shape represents a relationship in an ER diagram.

text: A primary key uniquely identifies:
options: A relationship instance
A single column value
Each record in a table
An entire database schema
explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each record (tuple) in a table.

text: In the network data model, the relationship between records is:
options: Many-to-many using sets
No relationships allowed
Hierarchical only structure
One-to-one relationships
explanation: The network model supports many-to-many relationships using sets (owner-member).

text: The relational data model was proposed by:
options: Edgar Dijkstra
Charles Bachman
E.F. Codd
Peter Chen
explanation: E.F. Codd proposed the relational data model in 1970.

text: Which of these is NOT a data model?
options: Relational model
Hierarchical model
Compiler model
Network model
explanation: There is no data model called the Compiler model.

text: In an Enhanced ER (EER) model, specialization is:
options: Adding new relationships between existing entity types
Combining multiple entity types into a single entity
The process of defining subclasses of an entity type
Removing attributes from entities during refinement
explanation: Specialization defines subclasses of a superclass entity type based on distinguishing characteristics.

text: A weak entity in an ER diagram:
options: Cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone
Is always fully independent of any other entity type
Cannot have any attributes or properties defined on it
Has a complete primary key defined on its own attributes
explanation: A weak entity depends on an owner entity for identification as it lacks a complete primary key.

text: Total participation in an ER diagram means:
options: The relationship is optional for all entities
No entity participates in the defined relationship
Only some entities participate in the relationship
Every entity must participate in the relationship
explanation: Total participation means every entity in the entity set must participate in the relationship.

text: A composite attribute is:
options: An attribute that holds multiple values for one entity
An attribute that serves as the key for identification
An attribute whose value is derived from other attributes
An attribute that can be divided into smaller sub-attributes
explanation: A composite attribute can be divided into smaller meaningful sub-attributes (e.g., Name into First and Last).

text: A multivalued attribute is represented in an ER diagram by:
options: A single ellipse
A rectangle shape
A dashed ellipse
A double ellipse
explanation: A double ellipse represents a multivalued attribute in an ER diagram.

text: In an ER diagram, a double rectangle represents:
options: A weak entity
An attribute
A relationship
A strong entity
explanation: A double rectangle represents a weak entity in an ER diagram.

text: Generalization in EER modeling is:
options: Adding new attributes to an existing entity class
Removing a class entirely from the ER model
Splitting a single class into multiple subclasses
Combining subclasses into a generalized superclass
explanation: Generalization combines common features of subclasses into a generalized superclass.

text: A derived attribute is one whose value:
options: Must be entered manually by users
Cannot change once it is set initially
Is always NULL in every record stored
Can be computed from other attributes
explanation: A derived attribute's value can be computed from other stored attributes (e.g., age from date of birth).

text: The cardinality ratio in an ER model specifies:
options: The overall size of the database measured in stored records
The number of attributes defined on a single entity type
The total number of entities present in the entire ER model
The number of relationship instances an entity can participate in
explanation: Cardinality ratio specifies the maximum number of relationship instances an entity can participate in.

text: An object-oriented data model supports:
options: Only tree structures and parent-child links
Only tables, rows, and column structures
Only flat files and sequential access paths
Encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism
explanation: The object-oriented data model supports OOP concepts like encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism.

text: In the EER model, an overlapping specialization means:
options: An entity can belong to multiple subclasses
No entity can be specialized at all
An entity belongs to exactly one subclass
Subclasses have no common superclass type
explanation: Overlapping specialization allows an entity to be a member of more than one subclass simultaneously.

text: A category (union type) in the EER model represents:
options: A superclass entity that has no subclasses
An entity defined without any attributes
A relationship with no participants at all
A subclass with multiple possible superclasses
explanation: A category is a subclass that is a union of different entity types (multiple superclasses).

text: The concept of aggregation in the ER model is used to:
options: Treat a relationship set as a higher-level entity
Create weak entities that depend on other types
Delete entities from the conceptual data model
Merge two databases into a single schema model
explanation: Aggregation treats a relationship set and its participating entities as a higher-level entity.

text: In a ternary relationship among three entities A, B, and C, the cardinality constraint is specified:
options: Only for the strongest entity participating here
Only for one entity in the ternary relationship
Cardinality does not apply to ternary relationships
For each pair of entities with respect to the third
explanation: In a ternary relationship, cardinality is specified for each pair of entities relative to the third.

text: Disjoint constraint (d) on specialization means:
options: An entity can be a member of at most one subclass
Subclasses can overlap with shared memberships
There is no constraint on subclass membership
An entity must belong to all subclasses at once
explanation: The disjoint constraint ensures an entity can belong to at most one subclass of a specialization.

text: Attribute inheritance in EER means:
options: Attributes are shared across all entities uniformly
Subclass entities inherit all attributes of the superclass
Attributes cannot be inherited between entity types
Superclass inherits all attributes from the subclass
explanation: Attribute inheritance means subclass entities automatically inherit all attributes defined for the superclass.

text: A lattice in the EER model occurs when:
options: An entity has no attributes or properties defined on it
A superclass has no subclasses defined in the model at all
A subclass has more than one superclass (multiple inheritance)
All entities in the model are weak entity types entirely
explanation: A lattice structure occurs with multiple inheritance where a subclass has more than one superclass.

text: The Object-Relational model extends the relational model by adding:
options: Hierarchical parent-child relationships between records
Network-style pointers linking records across the model
Only more tables and relations without other extensions
Support for complex data types, methods, and inheritance
explanation: The Object-Relational model adds complex types, user-defined types, methods, and inheritance to the relational model.

text: In the Entity-Relationship model, the participation constraint and cardinality ratio together define:
options: Query optimization parameters used
Physical storage structure of tables
Only the key constraints of entities
Structural constraints of a relationship
explanation: Structural constraints include both cardinality ratio and participation constraints of a relationship.

text: A recursive relationship is one where:
options: No entity type participates in the relationship that is defined
The same entity type participates more than once in different roles
Only weak entity types participate in the relationship instance
Two different entity types participate together in one relationship
explanation: A recursive (unary) relationship involves the same entity type participating in different roles (e.g., Employee manages Employee).

text: In a relational database, a table is also called a:
options: Tree
Graph
Document
Relation
explanation: In relational terminology, a table is called a relation.

text: A row in a relational table is called a:
options: Schema
Attribute
Domain
Tuple
explanation: A row in a relational table is called a tuple.

text: A column in a relational table is called:
options: An attribute
A relation
A key value
A tuple
explanation: A column in a relation is called an attribute.

text: A primary key must be:
options: Unique and not null
Duplicate in values
Foreign key based
Nullable and optional
explanation: A primary key must have unique values and cannot contain NULL values.

text: A foreign key is:
options: A key used only for sorting records in a particular order
The first column of any table regardless of its data content
An attribute that is always unique across all related tables
An attribute that references the primary key of another table
explanation: A foreign key is an attribute in one table that references the primary key of another table.

text: The domain of an attribute specifies:
options: The number of rows containing that attribute
The name assigned to identify that attribute
The set of allowed values for that attribute
The table name where the attribute exists
explanation: The domain defines the set of permissible values an attribute can take.

text: The degree of a relation refers to:
options: The number of tables in database
The number of tuples (rows) stored
The number of keys in the schema
The number of attributes (columns)
explanation: The degree of a relation is the number of attributes (columns) it has.

text: The cardinality of a relation refers to:
options: The number of keys in the table
The number of foreign key links
The number of tuples (rows)
The number of attributes (columns)
explanation: The cardinality is the number of tuples (rows) in a relation.

text: Which of the following is a property of a relational table?
options: Rows must be ordered
Each row is unique
Columns can be duplicated
Attributes are ordered
explanation: In a relation, each tuple (row) must be unique - no two identical rows are allowed.

text: NULL in a database represents:
options: An empty string character
A zero numeric value only
A space character in data
An unknown or missing value
explanation: NULL represents an unknown, unavailable, or not applicable value.

text: A candidate key is:
options: Any arbitrary attribute present in the table
The same concept as a foreign key reference
A minimal superkey that uniquely identifies tuples
Always the first column defined in the schema
explanation: A candidate key is a minimal set of attributes that can uniquely identify each tuple in a relation.

text: A superkey is:
options: Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies tuples
A key that is permitted to contain NULL values
Always limited to a single attribute in the table
The same concept as a foreign key in a relation
explanation: A superkey is any set of attributes whose values uniquely identify tuples in a relation.

text: Referential integrity constraint ensures that:
options: A foreign key value must match a primary key value in the referenced table or be NULL
All columns across every table in the database must have the same compatible data type
All tables in the database schema must have exactly the same total number of data rows
Primary key values can be duplicated across different rows within the same data table
explanation: Referential integrity ensures every foreign key value exists as a primary key in the referenced table or is NULL.

text: Entity integrity constraint states that:
options: Foreign keys must always be unique in tables
Tables must have at least one row of data
All attributes in every row must have values
No primary key attribute can have a NULL value
explanation: Entity integrity states that no component of a primary key is allowed to be NULL.

text: An alternate key is:
options: A composite key made up of two or more attributes
A candidate key that is not chosen as the primary key
A key that is permitted to contain NULL value entries
The same concept as a foreign key in another table
explanation: An alternate key is a candidate key that was not selected as the primary key.

text: A composite key is:
options: A key with only one single attribute value
A foreign key referencing another table
A key that allows NULL values in rows
A key consisting of two or more attributes
explanation: A composite key consists of two or more attributes that together uniquely identify a tuple.

text: Which of the following is NOT a relational integrity constraint?
options: Entity integrity
Sequential constraint
Referential integrity
Domain constraint
explanation: Sequential constraint is not a standard relational integrity constraint.

text: A relation schema defines:
options: Only the primary key definition of the relation in question
The structure of a relation including attribute names and domains
The actual data values currently stored within the relation
Only the foreign keys that reference other related tables
explanation: A relation schema specifies the structure: relation name, attribute names, and their domains.

text: In relational algebra, the Cartesian product of two relations R and S produces:
options: All possible combinations of tuples from R and S
The intersection of tuples in both R and S
Only tuples that match on common attributes
The union of all tuples from both R and S
explanation: The Cartesian product combines every tuple of R with every tuple of S.

text: A relation in 1NF requires that:
options: The table has only one column of values
There are no foreign keys in the relation
All values are strictly numeric in type
All attribute values are atomic (indivisible)
explanation: First Normal Form (1NF) requires that all attribute values be atomic (no repeating groups or multi-valued attributes).

text: Codd's Rule 0 (Foundation Rule) states:
options: SQL is the only query language allowed for use within the database system
Indexes are mandatory for every attribute present in all database tables
All data must be stored exclusively in flat files without any table structure
A relational DBMS must manage data entirely through its relational capabilities
explanation: Codd's Rule 0 states that the system must qualify as relational and manage the database through its relational capabilities.

text: The information rule (Codd's Rule 1) states that:
options: Pointers can be used to represent data relationships
All information must be represented as values in tables
Metadata need not be stored in tables or relations
Data can be stored in external files outside the system
explanation: Codd's Rule 1 states all information in a relational database must be represented explicitly as values in tables.

text: A relation is in BCNF if:
options: For every non-trivial functional dependency X to Y, X is a superkey
All attributes in the relation are prime (part of some key)
It has no multivalued dependencies among any of its attributes
It satisfies only the requirements of first normal form (1NF)
explanation: BCNF requires that for every non-trivial FD X→Y, X must be a superkey of the relation.

text: The closure of a set of attributes X (X+) under a set of functional dependencies is:
options: The set of all foreign keys that reference attribute X
The set of all attributes functionally determined by X
The total number of individual attributes within X
The set of all tables in the database containing X
explanation: The closure X+ is the set of all attributes that are functionally determined by X under the given FDs.

text: Armstrong's axioms include all EXCEPT:
options: Augmentation
Reflexivity
Distribution
Transitivity
explanation: Armstrong's axioms are Reflexivity, Augmentation, and Transitivity. Distribution is not one of them.

text: A lossless-join decomposition guarantees that:
options: The original relation can be reconstructed by joining the decomposed relations
No foreign keys are ever needed between the resulting decomposed relations
Join operations are always faster when applied to the decomposed relations
Some data will always be permanently lost during the decomposition process
explanation: Lossless-join decomposition ensures no information is lost when a relation is decomposed and later joined.

text: The dependency preservation property ensures that:
options: Only primary key dependencies are preserved during the decomposition of tables
All functional dependencies can be checked within individual decomposed relations
No dependencies are maintained after decomposition of the original relation
Dependencies are stored separately from the decomposed relation structures
explanation: Dependency preservation ensures each FD can be enforced by checking a single decomposed relation.

text: A surrogate key is:
options: A system-generated unique identifier with no business meaning
A natural key derived directly from the business domain
A composite key made up of multiple business attributes
A foreign key referencing a primary key in another table
explanation: A surrogate key is an artificial, system-generated key that has no inherent business meaning.

text: Codd defined how many rules for a fully relational database?
options: 12 rules (numbered 0-12, so 13 total)
10 rules (numbered 1 through 10 only)
5 rules (covering the basic properties)
20 rules (for comprehensive coverage)
explanation: Codd defined 13 rules (numbered 0 through 12) for a fully relational database system.

text: The Closed World Assumption in relational databases means:
options: Facts not present in the database are considered false
Nothing stored in the database is considered to be true
All possible facts about the domain are stored in it
The database cannot be modified once it has been created
explanation: The Closed World Assumption states that if a fact is not in the database, it is assumed to be false.

text: The selection operation in relational algebra:
options: Selects tuples (rows) that satisfy a condition
Selects specific columns from a given relation
Renames a relation or its attribute identifiers
Combines two relations into a single relation
explanation: Selection (σ) filters tuples from a relation based on a given condition.

text: The projection operation in relational algebra:
options: Deletes tuples from a stored relation
Selects specific columns from a relation
Joins two relations on common attributes
Selects specific rows from a given relation
explanation: Projection (π) selects specified attributes (columns) and removes duplicates.

text: The union of two relations R and S contains:
options: Only tuples that are common to both R and S
All tuples from both R and S without duplicates
The Cartesian product of relations R and S
Tuples in R but not present in relation S
explanation: Union (R ∪ S) returns all tuples that are in R, in S, or in both, without duplicates.

text: Which relational algebra operation renames a relation or its attributes?
options: Rename (ρ)
Projection (π)
Division (÷)
Selection (σ)
explanation: The rename operation (ρ) is used to rename relations or their attributes.

text: The Cartesian product of relations R and S is denoted as:
options: R ∪ S
R × S
R ∩ S
R − S
explanation: The Cartesian product is denoted as R × S and pairs every tuple of R with every tuple of S.

text: Set difference (R − S) returns:
options: Common tuples found in R and S
Tuples in S that are not in R
Tuples in R that are not in S
All tuples from both R and S
explanation: Set difference (R − S) returns tuples that are in R but not in S.

text: For union compatibility, two relations must have:
options: The same number of attributes with compatible domains
The same name for both relations being combined
The same number of tuples stored in both relations
Identical primary keys defined on both data relations
explanation: Union compatibility requires the same number of attributes with corresponding domains being compatible.

text: The intersection of R and S (R ∩ S) returns:
options: Tuples that are only in relation R
Tuples that are only in relation S
All tuples from relations R and S
Tuples that are in both R and S
explanation: Intersection returns tuples common to both relations R and S.

text: Which operation is fundamental in relational algebra (cannot be derived from others)?
options: Intersection
Division
Selection
Natural join
explanation: Selection is one of the fundamental operations; intersection can be derived from other operations.

text: The natural join operation:
options: Renames attributes within a relation for query convenience
Returns all tuples from both relations regardless of matching
Combines tuples from two relations based on common attributes
Removes duplicates from a single relation without conditions
explanation: Natural join combines tuples from two relations where the values of common attributes match.

text: The theta join of relations R and S is:
options: A rename operation applied to change the relation name
A Cartesian product followed by selection with a condition
A set union combining all tuples from two given relations
A simple projection on selected attributes from the relation
explanation: Theta join (R ⋈θ S) is the Cartesian product of R and S followed by selection based on condition θ.

text: An equi-join is a special case of theta join where:
options: No condition is applied to the join result
The result of the join is always an empty set
The condition uses only inequality comparisons
The condition uses only equality comparisons
explanation: An equi-join is a theta join where the join condition uses only the equality (=) operator.

text: The division operation R ÷ S returns:
options: The Cartesian product of both relations R and S fully
Tuples in R that are associated with every tuple in S
All tuples from relation S regardless of the condition
All tuples from relation R regardless of the condition
explanation: Division R ÷ S returns tuples from R that are associated with all tuples in S.

text: Left outer join includes:
options: All tuples from the right relation and matching from the left side
Only tuples that have matching values in both of the joined tables
No tuples at all if there is no matching condition between tables
All tuples from the left relation and matching tuples from the right
explanation: Left outer join preserves all tuples from the left relation, padding with NULLs where no match exists.

text: In relational algebra, which operation can be derived from the fundamental operations?
options: Projection
Intersection
Division
Selection
explanation: Intersection can be derived: R ∩ S = R − (R − S).

text: The semi-join of R and S returns:
options: All tuples from both relations R and S combined
The complete Cartesian product of R and S result
Only the tuples from relation S not in relation R
Tuples from R that have matching tuples in S
explanation: Semi-join returns only those tuples from R for which there is a matching tuple in S.

text: Anti-join of R and S returns:
options: All tuples from R regardless of matching in S
The complete Cartesian product of R and S both
Tuples from R that have no matching tuples in S
All tuples that match between relations R and S
explanation: Anti-join returns tuples from R that do not have a matching tuple in S.

text: Relational calculus is:
options: A procedural query language that specifies how to retrieve data
A data definition language for creating tables and constraints
A physical storage language for defining data file structures
A non-procedural query language that describes what to retrieve
explanation: Relational calculus is non-procedural; it specifies what to retrieve without specifying how.

text: Tuple relational calculus uses:
options: Tuple variables that range over relations
Domain variables that range over values
Only table names without any variables
No variables of any kind are required
explanation: Tuple relational calculus uses tuple variables that range over tuples of a relation.

text: Domain relational calculus uses:
options: SQL queries written in standard syntax format
No variables of any kind within the expression
Domain variables that range over attribute domains
Tuple variables that range over entire relations
explanation: Domain relational calculus uses domain variables that range over the domains of attributes.

text: A query language is relationally complete if it can express:
options: All queries expressible in relational calculus
Only join operations between related tables
Only aggregate functions on grouped data
Only selection and projection operations used
explanation: A relationally complete language can express any query that can be expressed in relational calculus.

text: The aggregate operation in extended relational algebra:
options: Performs Cartesian products between two unrelated relations
Renames relations and their associated attributes in queries
Selects individual tuples based on a simple filter condition
Computes functions like SUM, AVG, COUNT on groups of tuples
explanation: The aggregate operation applies aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.) to groups of tuples.

text: Which of the following cannot be expressed in basic relational algebra?
options: Projection of attributes from relations
Selection with various filter conditions
Recursive queries like transitive closure
Natural join of two compatible relations
explanation: Basic relational algebra cannot express recursive queries such as computing the transitive closure.

text: The concept of safe expressions in relational calculus ensures:
options: Queries always run faster than other approaches
No NULL values appear in the query results
All tuples in the relation are always selected
The result of a query is a finite set of tuples
explanation: Safe expressions guarantee that the result of a query is a finite relation, not an infinite set.

text: In tuple relational calculus, the expression {t | P(t)} means:
options: Update tuples in the relation where P is a false value
Delete all tuples from the relation where P is true value
The set of all tuples t such that predicate P(t) is true
Insert tuples into the relation that satisfy predicate P
explanation: The expression {t | P(t)} denotes the set of all tuples t for which predicate P is true.

text: The outer union operation:
options: Only works with relations that have identical schemas and matching attributes
Is the same as regular union and produces exactly identical results every time
Takes the union of relations that are not union-compatible by padding with NULLs
Removes all NULL values from the result set after combining both relations
explanation: Outer union combines partially compatible relations by padding missing attributes with NULLs.

text: Full outer join preserves:
options: Only tuples from the left relation with NULL for non-matching rows
Only tuples from the right relation with NULL for non-matching rows
All tuples from both relations, padding with NULLs where necessary
Only tuples that have matching values in both of the joined tables
explanation: Full outer join preserves all tuples from both relations, using NULLs for non-matching attributes.

text: The multiset (bag) version of relational algebra differs from set-based algebra in that:
options: It removes all duplicates
It allows duplicate tuples
It does not support joins
It only supports selection
explanation: Multiset relational algebra allows duplicate tuples, unlike set-based algebra which eliminates duplicates.

text: Which algebraic equivalence rule is used for query optimization?
options: Removing all joins from the query plan
Pushing selections down the query tree
Eliminating projections from the query
Reversing the order of query operations
explanation: Pushing selections down the query tree reduces the size of intermediate results, improving efficiency.

text: The assignment operation in relational algebra is used to:
options: Delete relations permanently from the database schema and storage system
Assign the result of a relational algebra expression to a temporary variable
Modify existing tuples by updating their attribute values in the table
Create new databases and define their schema structure from scratch
explanation: The assignment operation (←) stores the result of an expression in a temporary relation variable.

text: SQL stands for:
options: Standard Question Language
Structured Query Language
System Query Logic
Simple Query Language
explanation: SQL stands for Structured Query Language.

text: Which SQL statement is used to retrieve data from a database?
options: DELETE
INSERT
UPDATE
SELECT
explanation: The SELECT statement is used to retrieve data from one or more tables.

text: Which SQL clause is used to filter rows?
options: HAVING
ORDER BY
WHERE
GROUP BY
explanation: The WHERE clause filters rows based on specified conditions.

text: Which SQL statement adds new rows to a table?
options: UPDATE SET
INSERT INTO
ALTER TABLE
CREATE INDEX
explanation: INSERT INTO is used to add new rows to a table.

text: Which SQL statement modifies existing data?
options: DROP
INSERT
UPDATE
SELECT
explanation: The UPDATE statement modifies existing data in a table.

text: Which SQL statement removes rows from a table?
options: ALTER
DELETE
TRUNCATE
DROP
explanation: The DELETE statement removes rows from a table based on a condition.

text: Which SQL command creates a new table?
options: CREATE TABLE
NEW TABLE
INSERT TABLE
ADD TABLE
explanation: CREATE TABLE is used to create a new table in the database.

text: Which SQL command permanently removes a table?
options: DESTROY TABLE
REMOVE TABLE
DELETE TABLE
DROP TABLE
explanation: DROP TABLE permanently removes a table and all its data from the database.

text: The ORDER BY clause is used to:
options: Filter rows by value
Join tables on keys
Sort the result set
Group rows together
explanation: ORDER BY sorts the result set in ascending or descending order.

text: Which keyword is used to remove duplicate rows from a result?
options: FILTER
UNIQUE
REMOVE
DISTINCT
explanation: The DISTINCT keyword eliminates duplicate rows from the result set.

text: Which SQL clause is used to filter groups in a GROUP BY query?
options: WHERE
HAVING
ORDER BY
LIMIT
explanation: HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY, while WHERE filters individual rows before grouping.

text: An INNER JOIN returns:
options: All rows from the left table with NULLs for right
Only rows that have matching values in both tables
All rows from the right table with NULLs for left
All rows from both the left and the right tables
explanation: INNER JOIN returns only the rows where there is a match in both tables.

text: Which aggregate function returns the number of rows?
options: COUNT()
SUM()
AVG()
MAX()
explanation: COUNT() returns the number of rows that match a specified condition.

text: A LEFT JOIN returns:
options: The Cartesian product of both tables without any condition
All rows from the right table and matching from the left
Only rows that have matching values in both joined tables
All rows from the left table and matching rows from the right
explanation: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table, with NULLs for non-matching right table columns.

text: Which SQL constraint ensures a column cannot have NULL values?
options: CHECK
UNIQUE
NOT NULL
DEFAULT
explanation: The NOT NULL constraint ensures that a column cannot have NULL values.

text: The LIKE operator is used with which wildcard characters?
options: # and @
% and _
* and ?
& and ^
explanation: The LIKE operator uses % (any sequence of characters) and _ (a single character) as wildcards.

text: What does the IN operator do in SQL?
options: Checks if a value matches any value in a list
Performs an inner join between two given tables
Inserts data into a specified target table row
Indexes a column for faster search performance
explanation: The IN operator checks whether a value matches any value in a specified list or subquery.

text: Which SQL clause limits the number of rows returned?
options: LIMIT (or TOP in SQL Server)
GROUP BY (aggregation clause)
HAVING (group filter clause)
WHERE (filter condition clause)
explanation: LIMIT (MySQL/PostgreSQL) or TOP (SQL Server) restricts the number of returned rows.

text: A subquery is:
options: A query that modifies existing data
A query that creates new table objects
A stored procedure in the database
A query nested inside another query
explanation: A subquery is a SELECT statement nested within another SQL statement.

text: The BETWEEN operator selects values:
options: Within a given inclusive range
Outside a specified range only
That are NULL valued entries
Equal to a single specific value
explanation: BETWEEN selects values within an inclusive range (including both endpoints).

text: A correlated subquery is different from a regular subquery because it:
options: References columns from the outer query and executes once per outer row
Does not reference the outer query and runs completely independently
Is always faster than any other type of subquery in all situations
Executes only once regardless of how many rows the outer query returns
explanation: A correlated subquery references the outer query's columns and re-executes for each row of the outer query.

text: The COALESCE function in SQL:
options: Calculates the average of numeric values
Returns the last value in a specified list
Concatenates strings from multiple columns
Returns the first non-NULL value in a list
explanation: COALESCE returns the first non-NULL expression in the list of arguments.

text: What is the difference between DELETE and TRUNCATE?
options: TRUNCATE can have a WHERE clause to filter which specific rows should be removed from tables
DELETE is faster than TRUNCATE because it removes all rows without any logging of the changes
They are identical in function and there is no difference between them in any database system
DELETE can have a WHERE clause and is logged; TRUNCATE removes all rows and is minimally logged
explanation: DELETE is DML, can have WHERE, and logs each row deletion. TRUNCATE is DDL, removes all rows, and is minimally logged.

text: The CASE expression in SQL is used for:
options: Managing concurrent transactions
Dropping indexes from a table
Creating tables in the database
Conditional logic within a query
explanation: The CASE expression provides if-then-else conditional logic within SQL queries.

text: A CROSS JOIN produces:
options: An empty result set with no rows
The intersection of two data tables
Only rows matching between tables
The Cartesian product of two tables
explanation: A CROSS JOIN produces the Cartesian product — every row from the first table paired with every row from the second.

text: Which SQL statement is used to create an index?
options: CREATE INDEX
ADD INDEX
INSERT INDEX
MAKE INDEX
explanation: CREATE INDEX is used to create an index on one or more columns of a table.

text: The EXISTS operator in SQL:
options: Returns TRUE if the subquery returns at least one row
Checks for NULL values in a specified column only
Returns the count of rows matching in the subquery
Creates a new table based on the subquery results
explanation: EXISTS returns TRUE if the subquery returns one or more rows.

text: A self-join is:
options: A cross join of two tables
An outer join between tables
A join of a table with itself
A join without any conditions
explanation: A self-join joins a table with itself, typically using aliases to distinguish the two instances.

text: UNION vs UNION ALL in SQL:
options: They are identical in behavior and produce exactly the same result set
UNION ALL removes duplicates while UNION keeps all including duplicates
UNION removes duplicates; UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates
UNION is faster because it skips the deduplication processing entirely
explanation: UNION removes duplicate rows from the combined result, while UNION ALL includes all rows.

text: What does the NVL/IFNULL/ISNULL function do?
options: Replaces NULL with a specified value
Creates NULL constraints on columns
Deletes all rows containing NULLs
Counts NULL values in the column
explanation: These functions replace NULL values with a specified default value (syntax varies by DBMS).

text: A view in SQL is:
options: A stored procedure in the database server
A virtual table based on a SELECT query
A physical copy of a table stored on disk
A type of index on one or more columns
explanation: A view is a virtual table whose contents are defined by a SELECT query.

text: Which SQL command creates a view?
options: INSERT VIEW
MAKE VIEW
BUILD VIEW
CREATE VIEW
explanation: CREATE VIEW is used to create a virtual table based on a query.

text: A stored procedure is:
options: A precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database
An index structure built on one or more columns of a table
A type of view that shows data from multiple source tables
A constraint that enforces data integrity rules on a table
explanation: A stored procedure is a precompiled collection of SQL statements stored and executed on the server.

text: A trigger in SQL is:
options: A constraint that restricts the values allowed in a specific column
A type of view that displays filtered data from the underlying table
A manual query written by the user and executed on demand each time
A procedure that automatically executes when a specific event occurs
explanation: A trigger is a special procedure that automatically fires in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events.

text: What is a cursor in SQL?
options: A pointer to a table that references its location in data storage
A database object used to retrieve and process rows one at a time
A type of index structure created on one or more table columns
A constraint on a column that restricts the allowed data values
explanation: A cursor allows row-by-row processing of query results.

text: Which SQL statement is used to grant privileges?
options: PERMIT
ALLOW
ENABLE
GRANT
explanation: The GRANT statement assigns specific privileges to users or roles.

text: Which SQL statement removes privileges?
options: DENY
DISABLE
REVOKE
REMOVE
explanation: The REVOKE statement removes previously granted privileges from users or roles.

text: An assertion in SQL is:
options: A view that provides a virtual representation of the underlying data
A stored procedure that runs on a scheduled basis within the server
A constraint that specifies a condition the database must always satisfy
A type of trigger that fires automatically when data changes occur
explanation: An assertion is a general constraint that the DBMS checks and enforces on the database.

text: What is a transaction in SQL?
options: A type of view that provides a virtual representation of table data
A single SELECT query that retrieves data from the database tables
An index structure that speeds up data retrieval from large tables
A sequence of operations treated as a single logical unit of work
explanation: A transaction is a sequence of operations performed as a single logical unit of work.

text: The COMMIT statement in SQL:
options: Creates a savepoint within the current active transaction
Starts a new transaction and initializes the session state
Undoes all changes made in the current transaction entirely
Saves all changes made in the current transaction permanently
explanation: COMMIT permanently saves all changes made during the current transaction.

text: A materialized view differs from a regular view in that it:
options: Cannot be refreshed or updated at all
Physically stores the result set on disk
Is always virtual and never materialized
Does not use SELECT queries for data
explanation: A materialized view stores the query result physically on disk, unlike a regular view which is virtual.

text: Window functions in SQL are used to:
options: Delete rows from tables based on specified filtering conditions
Create new tables by defining their schema and column structures
Perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row
Modify table structure by adding or removing column definitions
explanation: Window functions perform calculations across a window of rows related to the current row without collapsing them.

text: The ROW_NUMBER() function in SQL:
options: Deletes duplicate rows from the result set of the current query
Returns the primary key value from the underlying data table
Assigns a unique sequential number to each row within a partition
Counts the total number of rows stored in the entire data table
explanation: ROW_NUMBER() assigns a unique sequential integer to each row within its partition.

text: A Common Table Expression (CTE) is:
options: A trigger that fires automatically in response to a data change
A stored procedure that encapsulates reusable database SQL logic
A permanent table that persists after the query has been executed
A temporary named result set used within a single SQL statement
explanation: A CTE provides a temporary named result set that exists for the scope of a single SQL statement.

text: The RANK() function differs from ROW_NUMBER() in that:
options: RANK() never produces ties in the ranking of any result set rows
RANK() assigns the same rank to ties and skips subsequent ranks
ROW_NUMBER() handles ties by assigning them the same rank value
They are identical and produce exactly the same results always
explanation: RANK() gives the same rank to tied rows and skips the next rank(s), while ROW_NUMBER() always gives unique numbers.

text: A recursive CTE is used for:
options: Querying hierarchical or recursive data structures
Creating indexes on one or more table columns
Optimizing joins between tables in the query
Dropping tables from the database permanently
explanation: Recursive CTEs are used to query hierarchical data like organizational charts or tree structures.

text: The SAVEPOINT statement in SQL:
options: Commits the transaction and makes all changes fully permanent
Starts a new transaction and initializes all session variables
Creates a point within a transaction to which you can roll back
Drops a table and removes it permanently from the data schema
explanation: SAVEPOINT creates a named point in a transaction to enable partial rollback.

text: An INSTEAD OF trigger:
options: Executes after the triggering action ends
Never executes regardless of the trigger
Executes before the triggering action runs
Executes in place of the triggering action
explanation: An INSTEAD OF trigger fires instead of the triggering DML action, often used with views.

text: The MERGE statement in SQL combines:
options: Only DDL operations such as CREATE, ALTER, and DROP tables
Only INSERT and DELETE operations without any UPDATE logic
Only SELECT operations for querying data from source tables
INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations based on a condition
explanation: MERGE performs INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE operations in a single statement based on matching conditions.

text: What is a sequence in SQL?
options: A type of trigger that fires on data modification
An object that generates sequential numeric values
An ordered view of data filtered by a set condition
A sorted table arranged by a specific column value
explanation: A sequence is a database object that generates a series of unique numeric values.

text: The DENSE_RANK() function differs from RANK() in that:
options: They are identical in their ranking behavior
DENSE_RANK() only works with string values
DENSE_RANK() skips ranks after ties occur
DENSE_RANK() does not skip ranks after ties
explanation: DENSE_RANK() assigns consecutive ranks without gaps, unlike RANK() which skips ranks after ties.

text: Lateral joins (LATERAL keyword) allow:
options: The right side of the join to reference columns from the left side
Only cross joins to be performed without any join filter condition
Joining tables without specifying any condition between the rows
Only inner joins to be used between the two tables in the query
explanation: LATERAL joins allow the subquery on the right to reference columns from the left side of the join.

text: The PARTITION BY clause in window functions:
options: Creates new tables from partitioned subsets of the original data
Divides the result set into partitions for function calculation
Deletes rows from specific partitions based on filter conditions
Physically partitions a table into separate storage file segments
explanation: PARTITION BY divides rows into groups (partitions) within which the window function is applied.

text: Dynamic SQL refers to:
options: SQL without variables or parameters of any kind
SQL statements constructed and executed at runtime
SQL that runs only during the compilation phase
Static predefined queries that never change at all
explanation: Dynamic SQL involves constructing and executing SQL statements programmatically at runtime.

text: The PIVOT operation in SQL:
options: Joins two tables together
Transforms rows into columns
Transforms columns into rows
Creates an index on data
explanation: PIVOT rotates row values into column headers, transforming data from row-oriented to column-oriented format.

text: SQL injection can be prevented by:
options: Using parameterized queries and prepared statements
Storing user passwords in plain text in database
Disabling authentication on the database server
Using dynamic SQL without any input validation
explanation: Parameterized queries / prepared statements prevent SQL injection by separating SQL code from data.

text: A user-defined function (UDF) in SQL differs from a stored procedure in that:
options: A UDF must return a value and can be used in SELECT statements
A stored procedure can be used directly within SELECT queries
A UDF cannot return any values to the calling SQL statement
They are identical in every way with no functional difference
explanation: UDFs must return a value and can be used in SQL expressions, while stored procedures may or may not return values.

text: The GROUPING SETS clause in SQL:
options: Groups all rows into one single group regardless of the criteria
Removes grouping and returns individual rows from the result set
Allows specifying multiple grouping combinations in a single query
Sorts the result set based on the specified column or expression
explanation: GROUPING SETS allows specifying multiple GROUP BY groupings in one query, equivalent to UNION of multiple GROUP BYs.

text: ROLLUP and CUBE in SQL GROUP BY are used for:
options: Generating subtotals and grand totals across multiple dimensions
Sorting data within each group by a specified column expression
Creating indexes on columns that are used in GROUP BY clauses
Deleting grouped data from the tables based on aggregate values
explanation: ROLLUP generates hierarchical subtotals; CUBE generates subtotals for all combinations of dimensions.

text: Temporal tables in SQL track:
options: Network latency and response metrics
Only the current state of stored data
Deleted tables and their old schemas
Historical changes to data over time
explanation: Temporal tables automatically track and store the history of data changes over time.

text: Normalization in databases is the process of:
options: Adding redundant data to speed up query processing
Deleting all data from every table in the database
Creating as many tables as possible in the schema
Organizing data to reduce redundancy and dependency
explanation: Normalization organizes data to minimize redundancy and undesirable dependencies.

text: First Normal Form (1NF) requires:
options: All attributes are restricted to numeric
No foreign keys exist within the tables
All attributes contain atomic values only
Tables have only one column of any data
explanation: 1NF requires that each attribute contains only atomic (indivisible) values.

text: A functional dependency X → Y means:
options: The value of Y uniquely determines the value of X
X and Y always contain the same identical values
X and Y are completely unrelated to each other
The value of X uniquely determines the value of Y
explanation: X → Y means that for each value of X, there is exactly one associated value of Y.

text: Redundancy in a database leads to:
options: Insertion, deletion, and update anomalies
Better data integrity and consistency
Faster queries and improved performance
Simplified database design structure
explanation: Redundancy causes anomalies: insertion (can't add data without other data), deletion (losing data), and update (inconsistency).

text: An update anomaly occurs when:
options: A table is created for the first time in the schema
A new row is inserted into an existing data table
The same data must be changed in multiple places
An index is dropped from a column in the database
explanation: Update anomalies occur when the same fact stored in multiple rows must all be updated consistently.

text: Second Normal Form (2NF) requires:
options: Only atomic values without any other constraints
1NF and no partial dependency on the primary key
No functional dependencies of any kind at all
All attributes must serve as keys in the table
explanation: 2NF requires 1NF and that every non-key attribute is fully functionally dependent on the entire primary key.

text: Third Normal Form (3NF) requires:
options: No attributes defined at all in it
Only 1NF without other constraints
2NF and no transitive dependencies
All attributes are primary key parts
explanation: 3NF requires 2NF and no transitive dependency of non-key attributes on the primary key.

text: A transitive dependency exists when:
options: Two keys depend on each other in a circular dependency
A key attribute depends on a non-key attribute directly
A non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute
No dependencies exist between any attributes at all
explanation: A transitive dependency exists when a non-key attribute functionally determines another non-key attribute.

text: A partial dependency exists when:
options: A non-key attribute depends on the entire composite primary key
A non-key attribute depends on part of a composite primary key
No dependencies exist between any attributes in the data table
All attributes depend on all keys defined in the relation schema
explanation: A partial dependency occurs when a non-key attribute depends on only a portion of a composite primary key.

text: Denormalization is:
options: Removing all tables and their data from the entire database
Intentionally introducing redundancy to improve read performance
Deleting all indexes to reduce overall storage requirements
Normalizing the schema to a higher normal form than before
explanation: Denormalization deliberately adds redundancy to a normalized design to improve query performance.

text: Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) is stricter than 3NF because:
options: Every determinant must be a candidate key
It allows partial dependencies on some keys
It requires no functional dependencies at all
It requires only atomic values in each field
explanation: BCNF requires that for every non-trivial FD X→Y, X must be a superkey, which is stricter than 3NF.

text: A relation in BCNF is always in:
options: 4NF
1NF
5NF
3NF
explanation: BCNF is stricter than 3NF, so any relation in BCNF is automatically in 3NF.

text: A multivalued dependency X →→ Y exists when:
options: Y is a primary key attribute that uniquely identifies every tuple
A set of values of Y is determined by X, independent of other attributes
X functionally determines Y through a standard functional dependency
No dependency exists between the attributes X and Y in the relation
explanation: A multivalued dependency means that for each X value, there is a set of Y values independent of other attributes.

text: Fourth Normal Form (4NF) requires:
options: No functional dependencies of any kind in the schema
BCNF and no non-trivial multivalued dependencies
All attributes serve as keys in the relation structure
Only 1NF without any additional constraints applied
explanation: 4NF requires BCNF and that there are no non-trivial multivalued dependencies.

text: A lossless join decomposition ensures that:
options: Some data is always permanently lost during the decomposition process
Joining the decomposed relations reproduces the original relation exactly
Extra spurious tuples are generated when joining decomposed relations
Only key attributes are preserved in the decomposed relation fragments
explanation: A lossless join decomposition guarantees the original relation is perfectly reconstructed by joining.

text: Which normal form deals with join dependencies?
options: Third Normal Form (3NF)
First Normal Form (1NF)
Second Normal Form (2NF)
Fifth Normal Form (5NF)
explanation: 5NF (Project-Join Normal Form) deals with join dependencies.

text: Armstrong's axiom of reflexivity states:
options: If X determines Y, then XZ determines YZ for any Z
If Y is a subset of X, then X determines Y
If X determines Y and Y determines Z, then X determines Z
If X determines Y, then Y also determines X always
explanation: Reflexivity: if Y ⊆ X, then X → Y (a set of attributes always determines any of its subsets).

text: Armstrong's axiom of augmentation states:
options: If X determines Y and Y determines Z, then X determines Z
If X determines Y, then Y also determines X in reverse
If X determines Y, then XZ determines YZ for any attribute Z
If Y is a subset of X, then X functionally determines Y
explanation: Augmentation: if X → Y, then adding the same attributes Z to both sides preserves the dependency.

text: The canonical (minimal) cover of a set of FDs:
options: Is always empty regardless of the original dependency set
Contains all possible FDs derivable from the original set
Has maximum redundancy among all its functional dependencies
Has no redundant dependencies and no extraneous attributes
explanation: The canonical cover is a minimal set of FDs equivalent to the original, with no redundancy or extraneous attributes.

text: A dependency-preserving decomposition ensures that:
options: All original functional dependencies can be enforced without joining decomposed tables
All tables in the decomposition must have exactly the same schema and attributes
Joins are never needed between any of the resulting decomposed relation fragments
Some functional dependencies are always lost during the process of decomposition
explanation: Dependency preservation means each FD can be checked within individual decomposed relations.

text: Fifth Normal Form (5NF) eliminates:
options: Join dependencies that are not implied by candidate keys
Multivalued dependencies among the stored data attributes
Functional dependencies between attributes in the table
All types of dependencies regardless of their categories
explanation: 5NF eliminates join dependencies that are not implied by candidate keys.

text: A relation is in Domain-Key Normal Form (DKNF) if:
options: Every constraint is a logical consequence of domain constraints and key constraints
It satisfies only the requirements of first normal form and nothing beyond that
All attributes in the relation must serve as keys for unique identification
It has no constraints of any type defined on its attributes or relationships
explanation: DKNF requires that every constraint on the relation is implied by domain and key constraints.

text: The synthesis algorithm for 3NF decomposition:
options: Always produces a BCNF decomposition that preserves all functional dependencies
Uses the canonical cover to create a dependency-preserving, lossless decomposition
Only works for binary relations with exactly two attributes in the schema
Removes all dependencies from the relation during the decomposition procedure
explanation: The synthesis algorithm uses the minimal cover of FDs to produce a 3NF decomposition that is both lossless and dependency-preserving.

text: It is possible to have a decomposition that is lossless but NOT dependency-preserving in:
options: BCNF decomposition
1NF decomposition
Any decomposition
No decomposition
explanation: BCNF decomposition guarantees lossless join but may not preserve all functional dependencies.

text: The chase algorithm is used to:
options: Create indexes on frequently queried columns
Optimize storage allocation across disk pages
Test whether a decomposition is lossless
Test whether a query is syntactically correct
explanation: The chase algorithm tests if a decomposition has the lossless join property by applying FDs to a test table.

text: A trivial functional dependency is one where:
options: The right side is a subset of the left side
The left side is empty with no attributes
No attributes are involved in the FD
Both sides are identical key attributes
explanation: A functional dependency X → Y is trivial if Y is a subset of X.

text: An extraneous attribute in an FD X → Y is an attribute that:
options: Can be removed from X or Y without changing the closure
Cannot be removed under any circumstance from the FD
Must always be present in the functional dependency rule
Is a primary key attribute that cannot ever be removed
explanation: An extraneous attribute can be removed from a functional dependency without altering the closure of the FD set.

text: Normalization theory is based on which mathematical concept?
options: Functional dependencies and their properties
Probability theory and statistical methods
Set theory only without other foundations
Graph theory only without other foundations
explanation: Normalization theory is fundamentally based on functional dependencies and their formal properties.

text: When BCNF decomposition is not dependency-preserving, the alternative is:
options: Go to 4NF which eliminates all multivalued dependencies from the relation
Accept 3NF which guarantees both lossless join and dependency preservation
Denormalize completely to avoid any decomposition of the original table
Use 1NF only which requires just atomic values in every attribute field
explanation: When BCNF doesn't preserve all FDs, 3NF is preferred as it guarantees both lossless join and dependency preservation.

text: A fully functionally dependent attribute means:
options: Removing any attribute from the left side of the FD destroys the dependency
The attribute has no dependencies of any kind on other attributes at all
The attribute is part of the key and contributes to unique identification
The attribute depends on a single column only in the current relation schema
explanation: Full functional dependence means the attribute depends on the entire left side and no proper subset of it.

text: ACID properties of a transaction stand for:
options: Atomicity, Concurrency, Isolation, Durability
Addition, Consistency, Integrity, Durability
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Dependency
explanation: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.

text: Atomicity means that a transaction:
options: Runs in isolation from all other transactions
Is either fully completed or fully rolled back
Is stored permanently on the disk after start
Can be partially completed and then paused
