explanation: Atomicity ensures a transaction is treated as a single indivisible unit — all or nothing.

text: Consistency ensures that a transaction:
options: Never fails regardless of the operations within
Is stored permanently on disk after the commit
Runs concurrently with other active transactions
Brings the database from one valid state to another
explanation: Consistency ensures the database remains in a valid state before and after a transaction.

text: Isolation ensures that:
options: Transactions are never allowed to execute concurrently
All transactions see each other's changes immediately
Only one transaction can exist in the system at a time
Concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other
explanation: Isolation ensures that concurrent transactions execute as if they were running serially.

text: Durability ensures that:
options: Data is stored only in volatile memory and not on stable disk
Changes can be undone at any time even after transaction commit
Committed transaction changes persist even after system failure
Transactions are temporary and their changes are never saved
explanation: Durability guarantees that once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanent.

text: The COMMIT operation in a transaction:
options: Pauses the active transaction
Makes all changes permanent
Undoes all current changes
Starts a new transaction
explanation: COMMIT makes all changes made by the transaction permanent in the database.

text: The ROLLBACK operation in a transaction:
options: Undoes all changes made by the transaction
Starts a new transaction in the session
Deletes the database and all stored data
Saves all changes and makes them permanent
explanation: ROLLBACK undoes all changes made during the current transaction.

text: A transaction that has not yet been committed or rolled back is called:
options: Committed
Active
Aborted
Terminated
explanation: An active transaction is one that is currently executing and has not yet committed or aborted.

text: Which of the following is a transaction state?
options: All of these: Active, Partially committed, Committed, Failed, Aborted
Only Aborted is considered a valid state for a transaction process
Only Active is considered a valid state for a transaction in a DBMS
Only Committed is considered a valid state for a transaction to be
explanation: Transaction states include Active, Partially committed, Committed, Failed, and Aborted.

text: A schedule in transaction management is:
options: A single SQL query executed against the database tables
A backup plan for recovering from a system level failure
A type of index structure for speeding up data lookups
A sequence of operations from one or more transactions
explanation: A schedule is a chronological ordering of operations from concurrent transactions.

text: A serial schedule is one where:
options: Only one transaction is allowed in the database system total
Operations are randomly ordered across multiple transactions
Transactions execute one after another without interleaving
Transactions execute concurrently with operations interleaved
explanation: A serial schedule executes transactions sequentially with no interleaving of operations.

text: A serializable schedule is:
options: A schedule whose result is equivalent to some serial schedule
A schedule where all transactions run simultaneously at once
A schedule that is always a serial schedule by definition
A schedule with no transactions present within its timeline
explanation: A serializable schedule produces the same result as some serial execution of the same transactions.

text: Conflict serializability is tested using:
options: Hash table (bucket-based structure)
B-tree index (balanced tree structure)
ER diagram (entity relationship model)
Precedence graph (serialization graph)
explanation: A precedence graph determines conflict serializability — if it has no cycles, the schedule is conflict serializable.

text: Two operations conflict if:
options: They belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one is a write
They are both read operations accessing the same data item from within different transactions
They belong to the same transaction and access different data items within the transaction
They access different data items from different transactions without any write operations
explanation: Conflicting operations are from different transactions, access the same item, and at least one writes.

text: A dirty read occurs when:
options: No read operation occurs between any concurrent transactions
A transaction reads data that it has written by itself before
A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction reads data that has already been fully committed
explanation: A dirty read occurs when a transaction reads data that has been modified by another transaction that has not yet committed.

text: A non-repeatable read occurs when:
options: No read operation is performed during the entire transaction
All reads from a transaction return the same consistent value
A transaction reads a value once and never accesses it again
A transaction reads the same item twice and gets different values
explanation: Non-repeatable read occurs when a transaction reads the same data item twice and finds it changed by another committed transaction.

text: A phantom read occurs when:
options: A transaction re-executes a query and finds new rows added by another transaction
A transaction reads no rows at all from the table during its entire execution
A transaction creates a table and populates it with data from another data source
A transaction deletes rows from the table and those changes are then committed
explanation: Phantom reads occur when a transaction re-reads and finds new rows that were inserted by another committed transaction.

text: The READ UNCOMMITTED isolation level allows:
options: No anomalies of any kind during transaction processing
Dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads
Only phantom reads but prevents all other read anomalies
Only non-repeatable reads but prevents all dirty reads
explanation: READ UNCOMMITTED is the lowest isolation level, allowing dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads.

text: The SERIALIZABLE isolation level provides:
options: Complete isolation from other transactions
Only dirty read prevention mechanism
Faster performance than other levels
No isolation between any transactions
explanation: SERIALIZABLE provides the highest isolation level, preventing all concurrency anomalies.

text: A cascading rollback occurs when:
options: No rollback is needed because every transaction completes without any failures
Only one transaction rolls back while all other transactions continue normally
One transaction's rollback forces other dependent transactions to also roll back
All transactions commit successfully without any errors during their execution
explanation: Cascading rollback happens when an aborted transaction causes other transactions that read its uncommitted data to also roll back.

text: View serializability is:
options: Not related to serializability in any way
Less restrictive than conflict serializability
The same as conflict serializability criteria
More restrictive than conflict serializability
explanation: View serializability is less restrictive (allows more schedules) than conflict serializability.

text: A recoverable schedule ensures that:
options: A transaction commits only after all transactions whose data it read have committed
All transactions must run serially with no concurrent execution allowed at all
Transactions can commit in any order regardless of their data reading dependencies
Rollbacks are never needed because all transactions always complete successfully
explanation: In a recoverable schedule, if transaction Tj reads data written by Ti, then Ti must commit before Tj.

text: A cascadeless schedule is one where:
options: All transactions must abort immediately
Transactions only read committed data
Transactions can read uncommitted data
No reads are allowed in any transaction
explanation: A cascadeless schedule avoids cascading rollbacks by ensuring transactions only read committed data.

text: A strict schedule requires that:
options: No transaction reads or writes data written by an uncommitted transaction
Transactions can freely read uncommitted data from other transactions
Commits are not required for transactions to be considered complete
Only serial schedules are allowed for all transactions in the system
explanation: A strict schedule prevents both reading and writing of data items written by uncommitted transactions.

text: The precedence graph for testing conflict serializability has:
options: Nodes for SQL statements and edges for their execution ordering
Nodes for transactions and edges for conflicting operation pairs
Nodes for data items and edges for transactions accessing them
No nodes or edges are used in this graph representation at all
explanation: The precedence graph has transactions as nodes and directed edges based on conflicting operations order.

text: Testing view serializability is:
options: NP-complete
Linear time
Logarithmic time
Constant time
explanation: Testing whether a schedule is view serializable is NP-complete, unlike conflict serializability which is polynomial.

text: The READ COMMITTED isolation level prevents:
options: No anomalies of any kind
All anomalies including phantoms
Only dirty reads alone
Dirty and non-repeatable reads
explanation: READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads but still allows non-repeatable reads and phantom reads.

text: The REPEATABLE READ isolation level prevents:
options: All anomalies including phantom reads
Dirty reads and non-repeatable reads
Only dirty reads from transactions
No anomalies of any kind at all
explanation: REPEATABLE READ prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads but may still allow phantom reads.

text: In a multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) system:
options: Transactions must be serial with no concurrent execution allowed at all
Multiple versions of data items are maintained for concurrent access
Only one version of each data item exists at any given point in time
Locks are always required for every read and write operation performed
explanation: MVCC maintains multiple versions of data items so readers don't block writers and vice versa.

text: A blind write is:
options: A committed transaction that has been successfully applied
A query optimization technique that reduces disk access
A read operation that is not followed by any write at all
A write operation without reading the current value first
explanation: A blind write occurs when a transaction writes a data item without reading its current value first.

text: Concurrency control ensures:
options: Larger storage capacity on the disk drives
Better network speed between client nodes
Faster hardware performance for the system
Correct execution of concurrent transactions
explanation: Concurrency control manages simultaneous transaction execution to maintain data consistency.

text: A lock in database concurrency control is:
options: A mechanism to control access to a data item
A backup method for protecting against loss
A type of query for retrieving stored data
A type of index for speeding up data search
explanation: A lock is a mechanism that restricts access to a data item during concurrent execution.

text: A shared lock allows:
options: Writing by multiple transactions to the same data item
No access at all to the locked data item by any party
Only one transaction to read the data item at any time
Multiple transactions to read the data item simultaneously
explanation: A shared (read) lock allows multiple transactions to read the data item concurrently.

text: An exclusive lock allows:
options: Multiple transactions to write to the same data item
Multiple transactions to read from the same data item
Only one transaction to read and write the data item
No operations at all on the locked data item by anyone
explanation: An exclusive (write) lock gives sole access to the data item for reading and writing.

text: A deadlock in database systems occurs when:
options: All transactions commit at the same time without any conflicts found
A single transaction is very slow in completing its data operations
Two or more transactions are waiting for each other to release locks
No transactions are running in the database system at the moment
explanation: A deadlock occurs when transactions form a cycle of lock dependencies, each waiting for another.

text: The Two-Phase Locking (2PL) protocol has two phases:
options: Lock phase and Unlock phase only
Growing phase and Shrinking phase
Read phase and Write phase only
Start phase and End phase only
explanation: 2PL has a growing phase (acquiring locks, no releases) and a shrinking phase (releasing locks, no acquisitions).

text: Which of the following can break a deadlock?
options: Adding more locks to the waiting transactions
Creating more indexes on the locked data items
Aborting one of the deadlocked transactions
Increasing the buffer size for the data manager
explanation: Breaking a deadlock typically requires aborting (rolling back) one or more of the involved transactions.

text: Starvation in concurrency control occurs when:
options: All transactions complete quickly without any delays or blocking
A transaction waits indefinitely because others keep getting priority
The database runs out of storage space on the physical disk
No locks are used by any of the transactions in the database
explanation: Starvation occurs when a transaction is repeatedly denied access to resources it needs.

text: A lock manager is responsible for:
options: Designing schemas for new data models
Creating tables in the database schema
Writing SQL queries for data retrieval
Granting and releasing locks on data items
explanation: The lock manager handles lock requests, grants, and releases for concurrent transactions.

text: The lock compatibility matrix shows:
options: How to create new tables in the database schema now
Which lock types can coexist on the same data item
The execution plans for all queries in the database
Index structures for optimizing data retrieval speed
explanation: The lock compatibility matrix indicates which lock modes are compatible with each other.

text: Strict Two-Phase Locking requires that:
options: No locks are needed for any transaction under this locking protocol
Only shared locks are used and exclusive locks are never acquired
All exclusive locks are held until the transaction commits or aborts
Locks can be released at any time during the transaction processing
explanation: Strict 2PL holds all exclusive locks until transaction commit/abort to prevent cascading rollbacks.

text: Rigorous Two-Phase Locking requires:
options: Locks are released immediately after each individual use
All locks (shared and exclusive) are held until commit or abort
Only exclusive locks are held until the transaction commits
No growing phase exists in this locking protocol at all
explanation: Rigorous 2PL holds all locks until commit/abort, ensuring strict serializability.

text: A wait-die deadlock prevention scheme:
options: Allows younger transactions to wait; older ones are rolled back
Allows older transactions to wait; younger ones are rolled back
Prevents all waiting by immediately aborting every transaction
Kills all transactions to resolve and prevent deadlock situations
explanation: Wait-die: older transaction waits for younger; younger transaction requesting a lock held by older is rolled back.

text: A wound-wait deadlock prevention scheme:
options: All transactions wait without any preemption at all now
No preemption occurs under any circumstances in the system
Older transactions preempt younger ones; younger ones wait
Younger transactions preempt older ones; older ones wait
explanation: Wound-wait: older transaction preempts (wounds) younger; younger requesting lock held by older must wait.

text: A deadlock detection algorithm uses:
options: A hash table index
A wait-for graph
An ER diagram model
A B-tree structure
explanation: The wait-for graph tracks which transactions are waiting for which; a cycle indicates a deadlock.

text: Timestamp-based concurrency control assigns:
options: Random access to data items without any order
Locks to all data items accessed by transactions
A unique timestamp to each transaction for ordering
Priority based on the size of each transaction
explanation: Timestamp ordering assigns each transaction a unique timestamp and uses it to determine serialization order.

text: In timestamp ordering, if a transaction tries to write a data item with a later read timestamp:
options: The data item gets deleted
The transaction is rolled back
The read timestamp is updated
The write proceeds normally
explanation: If a transaction's timestamp is earlier than the data item's read timestamp, the write is rejected and the transaction rolls back.

text: Optimistic concurrency control assumes that:
options: Conflicts are frequent and locks must always be acquired
All transactions conflict and none can proceed at once
No validation is needed at any point during processing
Conflicts are rare, so validation is done at commit time
explanation: Optimistic protocols assume conflicts are rare and validate transactions only when they try to commit.

text: The three phases of optimistic concurrency control are:
options: Start, Process, End
Read, Validation, Write
Read, Write, Commit
Lock, Execute, Unlock
explanation: Optimistic control has Read (execute without locks), Validation (check for conflicts), and Write (apply changes).

text: Lock granularity refers to:
options: The number of transactions in the system
The type of query being currently executed
The size of the data item that is locked
The speed of acquiring locks on the item
explanation: Lock granularity determines the size of the lockable unit: row, page, table, or database level.

text: Multiple Granularity Locking uses intention locks. An Intention Shared (IS) lock means:
options: No lower-level locks exist on any of the descendant nodes in the tree
The item cannot be locked by any transaction under any circumstances
A transaction intends to acquire shared locks on finer-granularity items
A transaction has exclusively locked the entire item and all subitems
explanation: IS lock indicates the transaction intends to set shared locks on descendant nodes in the lock hierarchy.

text: An Intention Exclusive (IX) lock means:
options: A transaction currently has a shared lock on the item and its subitems
No locks are needed for any data items under this locking protocol
A transaction intends to acquire exclusive locks on finer-granularity items
The transaction is aborted because it cannot acquire the needed lock
explanation: IX lock indicates the transaction intends to set exclusive locks on descendant (finer granularity) nodes.

text: A Shared Intention Exclusive (SIX) lock means:
options: No intention locks exist on any node in the hierarchy and all nodes are freely open
The node is shared-locked and the transaction intends to exclusively lock finer items
The node is exclusively locked by the transaction and no other can access it at all
The transaction is complete and all locks have been released from every node here
explanation: SIX combines a shared lock on the node with an intention to set exclusive locks on descendants.

text: In the Thomas Write Rule (modification to timestamp ordering):
options: Obsolete writes are ignored instead of causing rollback
All writes cause rollback regardless of timestamp order
Reads are ignored and only writes are processed by it
Timestamps are not used in this modified protocol
explanation: The Thomas Write Rule skips obsolete writes (where a newer write already exists) instead of rolling back the transaction.

text: The phantom problem in locking can be solved by:
options: Allowing dirty reads between queries
Using only row-level locks in tables
Index locking or predicate locking
Removing all indexes from the tables
explanation: Index locking or predicate locking prevents phantom tuples by locking the index range or predicate space.

text: In multi-version timestamp ordering (MVTO):
options: Only one version of each data item is maintained
Reads always block writes until they are completed
No timestamps are used for ordering transactions
Each write creates a new version of the data item
explanation: MVTO maintains multiple versions of data items; writes create new versions tagged with the transaction timestamp.

text: The wound-wait scheme is a preemptive protocol because:
options: Younger transactions always preempt older ones holding locks on data items
No rollback ever occurs because all transactions complete without conflict
An older transaction can force a younger one holding a needed lock to roll back
Only reads are preempted while writes always proceed without interruption
explanation: Wound-wait is preemptive because the older transaction 'wounds' (forces rollback of) the younger transaction holding the lock.

text: In tree locking protocol (for B+ tree indexes), a transaction must:
options: Use no locks at all when accessing data through the index structure
Lock children before parents when traversing the index tree structure
Lock a node before locking its children, and unlock parent before child
Lock all nodes simultaneously before accessing any data in the tree
explanation: The tree protocol requires locking parent nodes before children, following the tree structure top-down.

text: Lock escalation is the process of:
options: Converting coarse-grained locks into many more fine-grained locks
Converting many fine-grained locks into fewer coarse-grained locks
Adding locks continuously without ever releasing any of them
Removing all locks from the data items in the locked collection
explanation: Lock escalation converts multiple fine-grained locks (e.g., row locks) into a single coarse-grained lock (e.g., table lock) to reduce overhead.

text: Snapshot isolation provides each transaction with:
options: Only read access to the data without any ability to write modifications
No isolation at all between concurrently executing database transactions
Access to the latest uncommitted data written by other active transactions
A consistent snapshot of the database as of the transaction's start time
explanation: Snapshot isolation gives each transaction a consistent view of the database from its start time.

text: Database recovery is the process of:
options: Deleting all data from the tables in the entire database
Restoring the database to a correct state after a failure
Creating new tables and defining their column structures
Optimizing queries by choosing better execution plans
explanation: Recovery restores the database to a consistent state after a failure.

text: A transaction log (write-ahead log) records:
options: Table structures and their column definitions in the database schema
Only SELECT queries that have been executed against the database tables
User login information and authentication credentials stored securely
All changes made by transactions before they are applied to the database
explanation: The transaction log records all modifications (before and after values) for recovery purposes.

text: The redo operation:
options: Creates a new database from scratch without using any data from the previous state
Re-applies changes of committed transactions that may not have been written to disk
Undoes all changes of committed transactions and reverts to the original data state
Deletes the entire log file and removes all records of previous transaction actions
explanation: Redo reapplies committed transaction changes that may not have reached the disk before failure.

text: The undo operation:
options: Reverses changes made by uncommitted transactions
Creates a backup of the current database on disk
Optimizes queries for faster execution performance
Applies committed changes to the database on disk
explanation: Undo reverses changes made by transactions that did not commit before the failure.

text: A checkpoint in database recovery is:
options: A type of lock used to prevent concurrent access to shared data items
An SQL statement used to query data from one or more database tables
A point at which all modified data is written to disk and recorded in the log
A type of index structure built on columns for faster data retrieval
explanation: A checkpoint forces all modified buffers to disk and records a checkpoint entry in the log.

text: The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol requires:
options: Logs are only written to disk after the transaction has committed fully
No log is needed for any database operations under this recovery protocol
Data must be written to disk before the corresponding log records are made
Log records must be written to disk before the corresponding data changes
explanation: WAL requires that log records are written to stable storage before the actual database changes.

text: A system crash typically causes:
options: Loss of data in main memory but not on disk
No data loss of any kind after the crash
Loss of all data everywhere in the system
Loss of data on disk but not in main memory
explanation: A system crash loses volatile (main memory) data while disk data typically survives.

text: A media failure refers to:
options: Application crash caused by a software defect
Network slowness affecting query response times
Disk or storage device failure causing data loss
Memory overflow due to excessive data loading
explanation: Media failure involves damage to the storage medium (disk), causing loss of stored data.

text: Database backups are important for:
options: Reducing table size by removing unnecessary data rows
Recovering from media failures and catastrophic events
Creating new databases from the backed up data copies
Improving query speed and reducing execution latency
explanation: Backups provide copies of the database for recovery from media failures and disasters.

text: An incremental backup stores:
options: The entire database every single time
Only the database schema definitions
Only the changes since the last backup
Only the transaction log file entries
explanation: An incremental backup stores only the data that has changed since the last backup, saving time and space.

text: Deferred update (no-undo/redo) recovery technique:
options: Requires neither undo nor redo operations during the recovery process
Writes changes immediately and may need undo for uncommitted changes
Does not write changes to disk until commit, so undo is never needed
Does not use a log file for recording any of the transaction changes
explanation: Deferred update postpones writing changes to disk until commit, eliminating the need for undo operations.

text: Immediate update recovery technique:
options: Does not use a log file for any of the transaction record entries
Writes changes only after commit, requiring only redo operations
Requires only redo operations during the crash recovery process
Writes changes to disk before commit, requiring both undo and redo
explanation: Immediate update writes changes to disk before commit, so both undo (for uncommitted) and redo (for committed) may be needed.

text: Shadow paging recovery technique:
options: Maintains two page tables: current and shadow
Requires checkpoints at all times to function
Uses only a log file for the recovery process
Cannot handle any system crashes that may occur
explanation: Shadow paging maintains a shadow copy of the page table; the shadow page table is the consistent backup.

text: After a crash, which transactions need to be redone?
options: All transactions regardless of their commit state now
Committed transactions whose changes may not be on disk
Uncommitted transactions that were active during crash
Only aborted transactions that failed before the crash
explanation: Committed transactions are redone to ensure their changes are reflected in the database.

text: After a crash, which transactions need to be undone?
options: All transactions regardless of their state at the time of the crash
No transactions need to be undone during the recovery after a crash
Transactions that were active (not committed) at the time of crash
Committed transactions that completed successfully before the crash
explanation: Active (uncommitted) transactions must be undone to remove their partial changes.

text: The ARIES recovery algorithm stands for:
options: Automated Recovery and Integrity Enforcement System
Advanced Recovery and Index Enhancement System
Algorithm for Redo and Integrity Evaluation System
Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics
explanation: ARIES stands for Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics.

text: ARIES uses which three phases during recovery?
options: Scan, Parse, Execute
Analysis, Redo, Undo
Lock, Execute, Unlock
Read, Write, Commit
explanation: ARIES recovery has three phases: Analysis (determine what needs to be done), Redo, and Undo.

text: A log sequence number (LSN) is:
options: The total number of active transactions
A type of lock used in concurrency control
The size of the log file on stable storage
A unique identifier for each log record
explanation: An LSN uniquely identifies each log record and indicates its position in the log.

text: Fuzzy checkpointing differs from standard checkpointing in that:
options: No log entry is written to record the occurrence of the checkpoint
The database does not need to stop processing during the checkpoint
All processing must stop completely during the checkpoint operation
It occurs only once during the entire lifetime of the database
explanation: Fuzzy checkpointing allows transactions to continue during the checkpoint process.

text: Physiological logging means:
options: Log records describe changes at the page level with logical operations within the page
Only logical operation names are logged without any reference to the physical pages
Only physical byte-level changes are logged for each modification to the data pages
No logging occurs at all for any modification made to the database during processing
explanation: Physiological logging combines physical (page-level) and logical (operation-level) descriptions of changes.

text: In ARIES, the analysis phase determines:
options: The set of dirty pages and active transactions at crash time
Only committed transactions that completed before the crash
The hardware failure cause and the affected disk components
Only the last checkpoint that was recorded in the log file
explanation: The analysis phase scans the log from the last checkpoint to determine dirty pages and active transactions.

text: In ARIES, the redo phase:
options: Replays all logged changes from the earliest dirty page LSN forward
Starts from the end of the log and processes records in reverse
Only replays committed transactions and ignores uncommitted ones
Undoes all transactions that were active at the time of the crash
explanation: Redo in ARIES starts from the earliest LSN of dirty pages and replays all changes to restore the state at crash time.

text: In ARIES, Compensation Log Records (CLRs) are:
options: Checkpoint records that mark points of database consistency
Log records for committed transactions only in the system
Log records written during undo to record the undo actions
Index maintenance records for B-tree structure updates
explanation: CLRs are written during the undo phase to record undo actions, preventing re-undoing during repeated crashes.

text: The steal/no-force buffer management policy means:
options: Uncommitted pages can be written to disk (steal); committed need not be forced immediately (no-force)
Committed pages are always forced to disk immediately; uncommitted pages are never written to disk
Only committed pages are present on disk; uncommitted data remains exclusively in the memory buffer
All pages are always kept in memory; nothing is written to disk until the entire database shuts down
explanation: Steal allows writing uncommitted data to disk; no-force doesn't require committed data to be immediately written to disk.

text: No-steal/force policy would mean:
options: Uncommitted pages are never written to disk, and committed pages are always forced to disk
Neither undo nor redo is needed with this policy since both cases are eliminated
Uncommitted pages can be written to disk at any time before the transaction commits
Committed pages may not be on disk immediately and can remain only in memory buffer
explanation: No-steal/force eliminates the need for undo (no uncommitted data on disk) and redo (committed data always on disk).

text: The redoLSN in ARIES is:
options: The checkpoint LSN that marks the most recent consistency checkpoint
The last LSN that was recorded in the transaction log before the crash
The LSN from which redo must start (earliest recLSN among dirty pages)
The LSN of the last commit record written to the stable storage log
explanation: The redoLSN is the earliest recLSN in the dirty page table, indicating where redo must begin.

text: Write-ahead logging requires that log records be written to stable storage before:
options: The transaction starts its initial read operation
Any query is executed against the database tables
The corresponding data page is flushed to disk
The user submits the query to the database system
explanation: WAL requires log records to reach stable storage before the modified data pages are written to disk.

text: In ARIES, when a transaction is undone, the undo follows:
options: The commit order of other transactions in the same schedule
The table creation order defined in the database schema file
The lastLSN chain backward through that transaction's log records
A random order without any specific sequence or priority rules
explanation: Undo follows the chain of prevLSN pointers backward through the transaction's own log records.

text: Remote backup systems for disaster recovery provide:
options: A copy of the database at a geographically separate site
Better index structures for improving search performance
Improved normalization of tables to reduce data redundancy
Faster query processing through parallel execution plans
explanation: Remote backup systems maintain a database copy at a distant location for disaster recovery.

text: The concept of idempotent redo means:
options: Applying redo multiple times produces the same result as applying it once
Redo can only be applied once and cannot be repeated after that point
Redo changes the result each time it is applied to the database state
Redo is never repeated because recovery always succeeds on first try
explanation: Idempotent redo ensures that re-applying the redo operation produces the same state, important for crash during recovery.

text: An index in a database is used to:
options: Replace primary key usage
Slow down query results
Speed up data retrieval
Increase storage redundancy
explanation: An index provides a fast access path to rows in a table, speeding up data retrieval.

text: A primary index is built on:
options: An unordered file without sorting
A foreign key only in the relation
Any non-key attribute in the table
The primary key of an ordered file
explanation: A primary index is defined on the ordering key field of an ordered (sorted) file.

text: A dense index has:
options: An entry for every block in the data file
Only one entry for the entire data file
An entry for every record in the data file
No entries at all in the index structure
explanation: A dense index has one index entry for every record in the data file.

text: A sparse index has:
options: An entry for every individual record stored
An entry for each block or group of records
Entries only for deleted records in the file
No entries at all in the entire index file
explanation: A sparse index has index entries for only some records, typically one per data block.

text: A B-tree is a:
options: Linear list with sequential access
Balanced search tree used for indexing
Binary search tree with two children
Unbalanced tree without constraints
explanation: A B-tree is a balanced tree structure that maintains sorted data for efficient search, insertion, and deletion.

text: In a B+ tree, all actual data pointers are stored in:
options: All nodes equally
Leaf nodes only
Root node only
Internal nodes only
explanation: In a B+ tree, data pointers are stored only in leaf nodes; internal nodes contain only keys for navigation.

text: Hashing is used for:
options: Direct access to records using a hash function
Sorting data into a specific order on columns
Sequential access to all records in the file
Creating views on top of the base table data
explanation: Hashing computes a storage address directly from the key value using a hash function.

text: A secondary index is built on:
options: The ordering field of the file
The primary key field only
A non-ordering field of a file
A foreign key field only
explanation: A secondary index is created on a field that is not the ordering field of the file.

text: A clustered index means:
options: Data rows are stored randomly without any ordering
Data rows are physically stored in the index order
No physical ordering is implied by the index at all
Multiple indexes exist on the same column of data
explanation: A clustered index determines the physical order of data rows in a table.

text: Which data structure is commonly used for implementing indexes?
options: Linked list nodes
Stack structure
Queue structure
B+ tree structure
explanation: B+ trees are the most commonly used data structure for database index implementation.

text: In a B+ tree of order n, an internal node can have at most:
options: n+1 keys in the node
n keys and n children
n children and n-1 keys
2n children in total
explanation: A B+ tree of order n has at most n children (pointers) and n-1 keys in each internal node.

text: The height of a B+ tree with n records and order p is approximately:
options: p*log(n)
n * p
log_p(n)
n / p
explanation: The height is approximately log base p of n, where p is the order (fan-out) of the tree.

text: Static hashing has which disadvantage?
options: Fixed number of buckets may lead to overflow as data grows
It requires B+ trees to function as the base structure
It is too slow for any practical database use case at all
It cannot handle any data stored in the database tables
explanation: Static hashing uses a fixed number of buckets, leading to overflow chains when data grows.

text: Extendible hashing handles growth by:
options: Deleting old records to make room for new data entries
Using a B+ tree instead of hashing for the index data
Increasing the number of hash functions used for keys
Doubling the directory and splitting buckets as needed
explanation: Extendible hashing uses a directory that doubles in size and splits only the overflowing bucket.

text: Linear hashing handles growth by:
options: Using a fixed number of buckets without any changes
Splitting all buckets simultaneously at the same time
Splitting buckets in a linear order, one at a time
Never splitting any buckets regardless of the load
explanation: Linear hashing splits buckets one at a time in a predetermined linear order as the load increases.

text: A composite index is an index on:
options: A stored procedure reference
Two or more columns combined
A view of the base table data
A single column of the table
explanation: A composite (concatenated) index is built on a combination of two or more columns.

text: A covering index is one that:
options: Indexes all tables in the database regardless of whether they need indexing
Has no columns included in it and serves no purpose for query optimization
Covers only the primary key column and does not include any other attributes
Contains all columns needed to satisfy a query without accessing the base table
explanation: A covering index includes all columns referenced by a query, eliminating the need to access the actual table.

text: A bitmap index is most efficient for:
options: Primary key columns that uniquely identify each row
Columns with unique values that never repeat at all
Columns with high cardinality (many distinct values)
Columns with low cardinality (few distinct values)
explanation: Bitmap indexes work best for columns with few distinct values (e.g., gender, status) in data warehousing.

text: An index-organized table (IOT) stores:
options: Data separately from all index structures created
Data only in memory without disk-based persistence
Data directly in the index structure (B+ tree)
Data in flat files without any indexing structures
explanation: An IOT stores the entire row data within the B+ tree index structure, eliminating separate table storage.

text: The term fan-out in a B+ tree refers to:
options: The number of records stored in the entire data file
The maximum number of pointers (children) in a node
The number of leaf nodes at the bottom of the tree
The total height of the tree from root to leaf level
explanation: Fan-out is the maximum number of children (pointers) an internal node can have.

text: In a B+ tree, the minimum occupancy of internal nodes (non-root) is:
options: All pointers must be full
One pointer (child) as minimum
Exactly n pointers at all times
Ceiling of n/2 pointers (children)
explanation: Non-root internal nodes must have at least ⌈n/2⌉ children (pointers), where n is the order.

text: The global depth in extendible hashing indicates:
options: The total number of records stored across all the buckets
The number of buckets currently allocated in the structure
The number of bits used to determine the directory entry
The number of hash functions used for the key computation
explanation: Global depth is the number of bits of the hash value used to index into the directory.

text: Local depth in extendible hashing indicates:
options: The depth of the B+ tree from the root node to the leaf node level
The total number of collisions that have occurred in the hash table
The number of bits used to determine which bucket a record belongs to
The size of the directory that maps hash values to bucket addresses
explanation: Local depth of a bucket indicates how many bits of the hash value are used to determine membership in that bucket.

text: A hash join in query processing works by:
options: Partitioning both relations using the same hash function then joining matching partitions
Using an index on the join attribute to look up matching tuples from the inner relation
Sorting both relations on the join attribute first and then merging the sorted result sets
Using nested loops where each tuple of one relation is compared with every tuple of other
explanation: Hash join hashes both relations on the join attribute into partitions, then probes matching partitions.

text: R-trees are used to index:
options: Text data and documents only
Temporal data and timestamps
One-dimensional key values only
Spatial (multidimensional) data
explanation: R-trees are specialized index structures for spatial data, using minimum bounding rectangles.

text: A function-based index is created on:
options: A user role that defines access permissions and grants
A database name used to identify the schema information
A table name stored in the database system catalog data
The result of a function applied to one or more columns
explanation: A function-based index indexes the result of a function/expression on columns (e.g., UPPER(name)).

text: An inverted index is commonly used in:
options: Foreign key enforcement
Numerical computations only
Primary key lookups only
Full-text search engines
explanation: An inverted index maps terms to the documents/records containing them, used extensively in text search.

text: The order of a B+ tree affects performance because:
options: A higher order means more keys per node, reducing tree height and disk I/O
A higher order always increases the tree height and requires more disk access
Lower order is always better because smaller nodes fit in memory more easily
Order has no effect on performance and does not change disk I/O requirements
explanation: Higher order means more keys per node, resulting in a shorter tree and fewer disk accesses for searches.

text: Bulk loading a B+ tree is more efficient than individual insertions because:
options: It creates an unbalanced tree that must be rebalanced later
It sorts data first and builds the tree bottom-up, minimizing splits
It skips creating the tree entirely and uses a flat file instead
It uses no sorting and inserts records in their original order
explanation: Bulk loading sorts records first, then builds the tree bottom-up, avoiding many individual insert operations and splits.

text: A skip list can be used as an alternative to B+ trees for indexing because:
options: It provides O(log n) average search, insert, and delete with simpler implementation
It uses less memory than any other data structure used for indexing purposes
It is always faster than B+ trees for every type of database query and operation
It requires no randomization and is fully deterministic in its performance
explanation: Skip lists offer logarithmic average-case performance with a simpler implementation than balanced trees.

text: Query processing involves:
options: Only creating tables without any query functionality
Only writing queries without any execution or output
Only storing data without any retrieval functionality
Parsing, optimizing, and executing database queries
explanation: Query processing involves parsing the query, generating execution plans, optimizing, and executing.

text: A query execution plan describes:
options: The user who wrote the query and their access privilege details
The table creation date and the schema modification history
The sequence of operations the DBMS will perform to execute a query
The query syntax and grammatical structure in the SQL language
explanation: An execution plan specifies the strategy and operations the DBMS uses to execute a query.

text: Query optimization aims to:
options: Find the most efficient way to execute a query
Make queries longer and more complex to write
Increase the number of disk accesses required
Slow down query processing for all operations
explanation: Query optimization selects the most efficient execution plan from among alternatives.

text: The EXPLAIN command in SQL shows:
options: The database schema layout
The query execution plan
User access permissions
The table data content
explanation: EXPLAIN displays the execution plan chosen by the query optimizer for a given SQL statement.

text: A full table scan reads:
options: Only indexed rows stored
Only the last row stored
Every row in the table
Only the first row found
explanation: A full table scan reads every row in the table, which is costly for large tables.

text: Using an index for a query is generally:
options: Always slower than performing a full table scan
Faster than a full table scan for selective queries
The same speed as a full table scan on all data
Not recommended for any database query at all
explanation: Index access is faster for selective queries that retrieve a small percentage of rows.

text: Which join algorithm compares every tuple in one relation with every tuple in another?
options: Nested Loop Join
Sort-Merge Join
Index Nested Join
Hash Join method
explanation: Nested loop join compares each tuple in the outer relation with every tuple in the inner relation.

text: The cost of a query is typically measured in:
options: Number of SQL statements used
Lines of code in the procedure
Number of disk I/O operations
Number of tables in the schema
explanation: Query cost is primarily measured by the number of disk I/O (block transfer) operations.

text: Selectivity of a condition refers to:
options: The size of the database measured in total bytes
The number of columns selected in the projection
The fraction of tuples that satisfy the condition
The number of tables referenced in the SQL query
explanation: Selectivity is the ratio of tuples satisfying a condition to the total number of tuples.

text: A query optimizer can be:
options: Only cost-based approach
Rule-based or cost-based
Neither rule nor cost based
Only rule-based approach
explanation: Query optimizers can use rules (heuristics) or cost estimates, or both, to choose execution plans.

text: Sort-Merge Join works by:
options: Using a full table scan on both relations without any index use
Sorting both relations on the join attribute and then merging them
Creating a hash table on one relation and probing with the other
Using nested loops to compare every tuple in both the relations
explanation: Sort-Merge Join sorts both relations on the join attribute, then merges the sorted relations.

text: Hash Join works by:
options: Nested iteration comparing every pair of tuples from relations
Sorting both relations on the join attribute and merging results
Using indexes on both tables to find matching join attributes
Building a hash table on one relation and probing with the other
explanation: Hash join builds a hash table on the smaller relation and probes it with tuples from the larger relation.

text: An index nested loop join uses:
options: No index at all on either of the two relations being joined here
An index on the inner relation to find matching tuples efficiently
An index on the outer relation only for scanning its tuple values
A full scan of both relations without using any index structures
explanation: Index nested loop join uses an index on the inner relation's join attribute for efficient lookups.

text: Pushing selections down in a query tree:
options: Increases the size of intermediate results
Removes selections entirely from the plan
Has no effect on the intermediate results
Reduces the size of intermediate results
explanation: Pushing selections down applies filtering earlier, reducing the number of tuples in intermediate results.

text: Materialization in query processing means:
options: Not storing any intermediate results and processing everything in one single pass
Using only indexes to process the query without accessing the base table data
Computing and storing the result of each operation before passing it to the next
Only computing the final result without evaluating any intermediate operations
explanation: Materialization computes each operation's full result and stores it temporarily before the next operation uses it.

text: Pipelining in query processing means:
options: Processing operations one at a time in a strict sequential fashion
Storing all intermediate results on disk before the next operation
Passing tuples from one operation to the next as they are produced
Using only a single CPU core for all database query computations
explanation: Pipelining sends output tuples of one operation directly to the next operation without materializing.

text: The catalog statistics used by a cost-based optimizer include:
options: Only the table name without any other statistical information
Only the primary key definition of each table in the catalog
Number of tuples, number of distinct values, number of blocks
Only column names without cardinality or distribution details
explanation: Catalog statistics include relation size, distinct values, block count, and index details for cost estimation.

text: Heuristic optimization rules include:
options: Perform joins before selections and projections always
Always use Cartesian products first before other joins
Perform selections and projections as early as possible
Avoid using indexes for any data retrieval operations
explanation: Heuristics like performing selections and projections early reduce intermediate result sizes.

text: The join ordering problem in query optimization refers to:
options: Ordering rows within a single table by a specific column
Ordering columns in a table based on their data type size
Determining the most efficient order to join multiple tables
Sorting query results in ascending or descending sequence
explanation: Join ordering determines which sequence of joins produces the lowest cost for multi-table queries.

text: Dynamic programming is used in query optimization for:
options: Creating tables and defining column data types
Parsing SQL queries into abstract syntax trees
Managing locks in the concurrency control layer
Finding the optimal join order for multiple tables
explanation: Dynamic programming efficiently explores all possible join orders to find the optimal plan.

text: The Selinger optimizer (System R) uses:
options: Only heuristic rules without any cost estimation being performed
Random plan selection from all possible execution plan choices
Dynamic programming with interesting orders to find optimal plans
No optimization at all with queries running as they are written
explanation: The System R optimizer uses dynamic programming considering 'interesting orders' (sort orders useful for later operations).

text: An interesting sort order in query optimization is:
options: Any random sort order applied to tuples without considering the query operations
A sort order used only for displaying the final result to the end user query
A sort order that increases cost by requiring additional sorting in later steps
A sort order produced by one operation that is useful for a subsequent operation
explanation: An interesting order is a tuple ordering produced by one operation that benefits a later operation (e.g., sort-merge join).

text: The number of possible join orders for n relations is:
options: Grows exponentially (Catalan number)
Always n squared for n relations
Always n for n given relations total
Exactly n! for n relations total
explanation: The number of possible join trees grows exponentially; for n relations it is related to Catalan numbers (super-exponential).

text: In cost-based optimization, the estimated cost of a nested loop join R ⋈ S is approximately:
options: Always 1 block access regardless of the sizes of relations R and S used
b_R plus b_S (sum of blocks from both R and S relations) in simple analysis
n_R plus n_S (sum of tuples from both R and S relations) in simple analysis
n_R times b_S plus b_R (b is blocks, n_R is tuples in R) in simple analysis
explanation: Simple nested loop join cost is approximately b_R + n_R * b_S block accesses (outer tuples times inner blocks plus outer blocks).

text: Histogram statistics improve query optimization by:
options: Increasing query complexity by adding more steps to the optimization process
Providing more accurate selectivity estimates for non-uniform data distributions
Removing the need for indexes by providing direct access to all stored records
Making all estimates uniform regardless of the actual data value distribution
explanation: Histograms capture the distribution of values, allowing better selectivity estimates than assuming uniform distribution.

text: A left-deep join tree is preferred by many optimizers because:
options: It is the only valid tree shape for all joins
It avoids all disk I/O during the join process
It minimizes the number of tables in joins
It allows pipelining of intermediate results
explanation: Left-deep trees allow pipelining of join results, avoiding materialization of intermediate results to disk.

text: Adaptive query processing differs from traditional optimization by:
options: Only optimizing before execution begins and then running without changes
Adjusting the execution plan during query execution based on runtime statistics
Never changing the plan once it has been selected by the query optimizer
Ignoring all statistics and using a fixed plan for every query submitted
explanation: Adaptive processing monitors runtime conditions and can modify the execution plan during execution.

text: The concept of equivalent query expressions means:
options: Identical query text that is written exactly the same way in SQL
Different relational algebra expressions that produce the same result
Queries that produce different results from the same input relations
Queries executed on different databases with different table schemas
explanation: Equivalent expressions produce the same result set, allowing the optimizer to choose the most efficient form.

text: Predicate pushdown in distributed queries:
options: Ignores remote data sources completely and only queries local tables
Sends all data to a central location first before any filtering occurs
Sends filter conditions to remote data sources to reduce data transfer
Removes all predicates from the query and returns all unfiltered data
explanation: Predicate pushdown sends WHERE conditions to remote sources so they filter data before sending it, reducing network transfer.

text: Columnar storage benefits analytical queries because:
options: No indexes are needed for any query on columnar data store
Row storage is used instead of column-oriented data format
All columns are always read regardless of the query needs
Only the needed columns are read from disk, reducing I/O
explanation: Columnar storage reads only the columns referenced by a query, significantly reducing disk I/O for analytical workloads.

text: Database security aims to protect the database from:
options: Faster queries and improved execution performance
Unauthorized access, modification, and destruction
Larger storage capacity on the available disk space
Better indexing and optimized data retrieval speed
explanation: Database security protects data integrity, confidentiality, and availability from unauthorized activities.

text: Authentication in database security verifies:
options: The identity of a user
The number of indexes
The speed of queries
The size of tables
explanation: Authentication verifies that a user is who they claim to be.

text: Authorization in database security determines:
options: The operating system and hardware details
What actions a user is permitted to perform
The database version and release number
The user password and login credentials
explanation: Authorization determines the privileges and operations a user is allowed to perform.

text: The SQL GRANT statement is used to:
options: Create tables in database
Remove privileges from users
Delete data from tables
Give privileges to users
explanation: GRANT assigns specific database privileges to users or roles.

text: The SQL REVOKE statement is used to:
options: Remove previously granted privileges
Insert data into existing table records
Grant new privileges to database users
Create views on top of the base tables
explanation: REVOKE removes privileges that were previously granted to users or roles.

text: A database role is:
options: An index structure built on columns for faster data retrieval
A type of table used to store data records within the database
A named collection of privileges that can be assigned to users
A query optimization technique to improve execution planning
explanation: A role groups privileges together, making it easier to manage permissions for multiple users.

text: Encryption in database security is used to:
options: Compress data to reduce the amount of storage space it requires
Create indexes on columns for faster retrieval of requested records
Convert data into an unreadable format to prevent unauthorized access
Speed up queries by creating cached copies of frequently used results
explanation: Encryption transforms data into ciphertext, making it unreadable without the proper decryption key.

text: An audit trail in a database records:
options: All operations performed on the database for security monitoring
Only DDL operations like CREATE, ALTER, and DROP table commands
Only user logins and their session authentication event details
Only SELECT queries that have been executed against stored tables
explanation: An audit trail logs database operations to track who did what and when.

text: SQL injection is a type of:
options: Indexing strategy used for speeding up data retrieval from the tables
Security attack that exploits vulnerabilities in SQL query construction
Database optimization technique that improves overall query performance
Backup method used for creating copies of the database for recovery
explanation: SQL injection is an attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into queries through user input.

text: The principle of least privilege means:
options: All users should have full access to every table in the database
Users should have only the minimum privileges needed for their tasks
No privileges should be granted to any user in the database
Only administrators should be allowed to use the database system
explanation: Least privilege grants users only the permissions necessary to perform their specific duties.

text: Mandatory Access Control (MAC) is based on:
options: Security labels and clearance levels assigned to subjects and objects
User discretion in deciding who can access which database objects
No access control at all with every user having full open access
Role assignments only without any additional security label checks
explanation: MAC uses security labels (classifications) on data objects and clearance levels on subjects to control access.

text: Discretionary Access Control (DAC) allows:
options: Data owners to grant and revoke access to other users
No access control with all data open to every user
Access based on security clearance only in the system
Only the DBA to control access to all database objects
explanation: DAC allows data owners to control access to their data by granting/revoking privileges at their discretion.

text: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) assigns privileges to:
options: Individual users only without grouping
Queries only without role management
Tables only without any user control
Roles, which are then assigned to users
explanation: RBAC assigns privileges to roles, and users are assigned to appropriate roles.

text: The Bell-LaPadula model enforces:
options: Only read access controls without write restrictions
No read-up and no write-down rules for confidentiality
No rules at all for controlling information access
Only write access controls without read restrictions
explanation: Bell-LaPadula enforces 'no read up' (simple security) and 'no write down' (star property) for confidentiality.

text: Data masking in database security:
options: Encrypts entire databases for secure data storage
Deletes all data from the tables in the database
Replaces sensitive data with realistic but fake data
Creates copies of data for backup and recovery use
explanation: Data masking replaces sensitive data with fictitious but realistic data for non-production environments.

text: A view can provide security by:
options: Deleting sensitive records from the database tables
Encrypting all data stored in the underlying tables
Creating new tables with restricted column schemas
Restricting which columns and rows a user can see
explanation: Views can restrict access to specific columns and rows, providing a security layer over base tables.

text: Virtual Private Database (VPD) provides:
options: Only audit logging without any enforcement of access policies
Column-level encryption only without any row-level restrictions
No security features of any kind for the database application
Row-level security by automatically adding conditions to queries
explanation: VPD automatically appends security predicates to queries, filtering rows based on user context.

text: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts:
options: Query results only sent to clients
Log files only on the disk storage
Data in transit only on the network
Data at rest on the storage media
explanation: TDE encrypts database files on disk (at rest) transparently, without requiring application changes.

text: Database activity monitoring (DAM) is used to:
options: Optimize storage allocation across available disk partitions
Speed up queries by caching frequently accessed data in memory
Create backups of the database on a scheduled periodic basis
Monitor and record database activity in real-time for security
explanation: DAM solutions monitor database traffic in real-time to detect unauthorized activities and policy violations.

text: The GRANT OPTION in SQL allows:
options: Creating tables and views in the database schema at will
Revoking all privileges from every user in the database
Deleting databases and all their data from the server
A grantee to further grant the same privilege to other users
explanation: GRANT OPTION allows the recipient of a privilege to pass that privilege on to other users.

text: Statistical database security addresses the problem of:
options: Managing user roles and their assigned privileges set
Creating backup copies of the database for recovery
Inferring individual data from aggregate query results
Encrypting data at rest on the physical storage media
explanation: Statistical database security prevents users from deducing individual values through carefully crafted aggregate queries.

text: The inference problem in database security occurs when:
options: No queries are allowed to be executed against the tables
Users deduce restricted information from permitted queries
Users have direct database access without any restrictions
All data is encrypted and cannot be read without the key
explanation: The inference problem occurs when users can deduce confidential information by combining permitted query results.

text: Label-based security in databases assigns:
options: Sensitivity labels to data rows and clearance levels to users
Labels only to tables without any row-level classification
No labels to any data regardless of its sensitivity or nature
Random access permissions to all users without any restrictions
explanation: Label-based security assigns sensitivity labels to individual rows and clearance levels to users for fine-grained access control.

text: Homomorphic encryption allows:
options: Faster decryption of data using optimized algorithms
Only reading encrypted data without any modifications
Computations on encrypted data without decrypting it
Only writing encrypted data without reading it first
explanation: Homomorphic encryption enables performing computations on ciphertext, producing encrypted results that match the plaintext computation.

text: Database firewall works by:
options: Encrypting all data stored in the database tables and log files
Only blocking network traffic without inspecting SQL statements
Creating physical firewalls around the database server hardware
Monitoring SQL traffic and blocking unauthorized or suspicious queries
explanation: A database firewall monitors SQL queries in real-time and blocks those that violate security policies.

text: The k-anonymity privacy model ensures:
options: Only k users can access the database at any given time during concurrent operations
Data is stored in k copies across different sites for redundancy and availability
All data is encrypted using a key of length k bits for secure storage and retrieval
Each record is indistinguishable from at least k-1 other records on quasi-identifiers
explanation: K-anonymity ensures that each record shares quasi-identifier values with at least k-1 other records to prevent re-identification.

text: Differential privacy provides:
options: Full access to all data without any restrictions or privacy controls
Only physical security measures without any mathematical foundations
No privacy guarantees of any kind for the data stored in the database
Mathematical guarantees that query results do not reveal individual data
explanation: Differential privacy adds controlled noise to query results to provide mathematical privacy guarantees for individuals.

text: A privilege graph is used to:
options: Track the flow of granted privileges among users
Design ER diagrams for conceptual data modeling
Optimize query execution plans for better speed
Create indexes on columns for fast data retrieval
explanation: A privilege graph shows how privileges flow from grantor to grantee, useful for cascade revocation analysis.

text: Cascading revocation in SQL means:
options: Revoking a privilege also revokes it from all users who received it through the original grant
No revocation occurs and the privilege remains active for all users who currently have it
Only the specified user loses the privilege and no other users are affected by the revocation
All database privileges for every user are revoked regardless of the specific grant involved
explanation: With CASCADE, revoking a privilege also revokes it from all users who received it transitively through that grant.

text: Column-level encryption differs from TDE in that:
options: It encrypts specific columns rather than entire database files
It encrypts only at the network level rather than at storage level
It encrypts only table names rather than the actual column data
It is less secure than TDE because it covers fewer data elements
explanation: Column-level encryption selectively encrypts individual columns containing sensitive data, while TDE encrypts entire database files.

text: A distributed database is:
options: A database stored only in the cloud without any local presence
A database stored on a single machine in one physical location
A database spread across multiple sites connected by a network
A backup copy of a database kept for disaster recovery purposes
explanation: A distributed database has data distributed across multiple sites connected by a communication network.

text: Data fragmentation in distributed databases means:
options: Duplicating the entire database at every site simultaneously
Compressing data to reduce storage requirements at each site
Losing data during transfer between sites on the network link
Dividing a relation into smaller parts stored at different sites
explanation: Fragmentation divides a relation into fragments that are stored at different sites.

text: Horizontal fragmentation divides a relation by:
options: Rows and columns both
Rows (tuples) only
Neither rows nor cols
Columns (attributes)
explanation: Horizontal fragmentation splits a relation into subsets of tuples (rows).

text: Vertical fragmentation divides a relation by:
options: Rows (tuples) only
Columns (attributes)
Neither of the above
Columns and rows both
explanation: Vertical fragmentation splits a relation into subsets of attributes (columns), including the key in each fragment.

text: Data replication in distributed databases means:
options: Compressing data to reduce transfer size
Deleting data from all distributed sites
Encrypting data before sending it across
Storing copies of data at multiple sites
explanation: Replication stores copies of data fragments at multiple sites for availability and performance.

text: The transparency goal in distributed databases means:
options: Data cannot be distributed across multiple sites
Users must know all data locations before querying
Only administrators can query distributed data
Users should not be aware that data is distributed
explanation: Transparency hides the distribution details from users, making the system appear as a single database.

text: Location transparency means:
options: Data is always stored locally on the user's own machine
Users must specify the exact data location in every query
Users do not need to know where data is physically stored
No remote access is possible from any site in the system
explanation: Location transparency allows users to access data without knowing its physical location.

text: A distributed DBMS (DDBMS) manages:
options: Only local databases without any distribution
A database distributed across multiple sites
Only a centralized database on one machine
Only cloud databases hosted by third parties
explanation: A DDBMS manages the storage and retrieval of data across multiple networked sites.

text: Which of the following is an advantage of distributed databases?
options: Slower queries being preferred by users
Higher costs being beneficial overall
Improved reliability and availability
Increased complexity as an advantage
explanation: Distributed databases offer improved reliability (no single point of failure) and availability.

text: Fragmentation transparency means:
options: Users are unaware that data is fragmented
Data cannot be fragmented at any site
Only fragments can be queried directly
Users must reassemble fragments manually
explanation: Fragmentation transparency allows users to query data without knowing about its fragmentation.

text: The two-phase commit (2PC) protocol ensures:
options: Only read operations complete
No atomicity guarantees at all
Only local transaction atomicity
Atomicity of distributed transactions
explanation: 2PC ensures that a distributed transaction either commits at all sites or aborts at all sites.

text: In the 2PC protocol, the coordinator first sends:
options: A prepare (vote) message to all participants
A commit message directly to all the sites
An abort message to cancel the transaction
A query to all participants for data first
explanation: In phase 1, the coordinator sends a prepare/vote-request message to all participant sites.

text: If any participant votes No in 2PC, the coordinator:
options: Ignores the vote and proceeds with commit
Sends an abort message to all participants
Retries the transaction automatically again
Sends a commit message to all participants
explanation: If any participant votes No (cannot commit), the coordinator sends a global abort to all participants.

text: A distributed query involves:
options: No data access from any site at all
Only local data access on one site
Accessing data from a single table
Accessing data from multiple sites
explanation: A distributed query requires accessing and combining data from tables stored at different sites.

text: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most:
options: Two of three: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance
None of these properties can be guaranteed in any system
Only one property out of the three possible in the theorem
All three properties at the same time without any trade-offs
explanation: The CAP theorem (Brewer's theorem) states that it's impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three properties.

text: Semi-join optimization in distributed queries:
options: Increases data transfer by sending all records across the link
Uses full Cartesian products between all fragments in the join
Reduces data transfer by sending only relevant join attributes
Eliminates all joins from the distributed query execution plan
explanation: Semi-join reduces network data transfer by first sending only the join attributes to filter matching tuples.

text: Mixed fragmentation combines:
options: No fragmentation is used at all
Only vertical fragmentation types
Horizontal and vertical fragmentation
Only horizontal fragmentation types
explanation: Mixed (hybrid) fragmentation applies both horizontal and vertical fragmentation strategies.

text: Full replication means:
options: Every site has a complete copy of the entire database
No replication exists at any site in the system at all
Only one site has the data and others have no copies
Data is partially replicated at only a few of sites
explanation: Full replication stores a complete copy of the database at every site.

text: The blocking problem in 2PC occurs when:
options: No failures occur during the execution of the two-phase commit protocol
The coordinator fails after sending prepare but before sending the decision
All participants commit successfully without any failures in the protocol
The network is fast and reliable with no message delays or lost packets
explanation: Blocking occurs when the coordinator crashes after prepare, leaving participants uncertain about whether to commit or abort.

text: Distributed deadlock detection is more complex because:
options: All transactions are local and never span multiple sites
Deadlocks can involve transactions at multiple sites
Deadlocks never occur in distributed database systems
Only one site has transactions running at any time
explanation: Distributed deadlocks span multiple sites, requiring global wait-for graph construction or timeout-based detection.

text: The three-phase commit (3PC) protocol improves on 2PC by:
options: Using only one phase for the commit decision
Removing the prepare phase from the protocol
Adding a pre-commit phase to avoid blocking
Ignoring all failures that occur at any site
explanation: 3PC adds a pre-commit phase between prepare and commit, avoiding the blocking problem of 2PC under certain failures.

text: In distributed databases, the global query optimization must consider:
options: Only CPU time without considering disk or network access costs
Only disk I/O costs without considering network or CPU overhead
Only local processing costs without considering network overhead
Network communication costs in addition to local processing costs
explanation: Distributed query optimization must account for network transfer costs, which can dominate total cost.

text: Paxos is a protocol used for:
options: Achieving consensus in distributed systems
Indexing data for faster retrieval queries
Query optimization in database management
Schema design for database normalization
explanation: Paxos is a consensus protocol that enables distributed nodes to agree on a value despite failures.

text: The RAFT consensus algorithm is designed to be:
options: Faster than all other protocols in every distributed environment
A replacement for SQL in distributed relational database systems
More understandable than Paxos while providing the same guarantees
A type of index for organizing data in distributed hash tables
explanation: RAFT was designed as a more understandable alternative to Paxos for distributed consensus.

text: In a federated database system:
options: No integration is possible between any databases at all
Autonomous databases cooperate to provide integrated access
Only one database exists in the entire distributed system
All databases are identical copies of the same data schema
explanation: A federated database system integrates multiple autonomous databases while preserving their independence.

text: The eventual consistency model guarantees that:
options: No consistency is provided between any of the replicas in the system
Immediate consistency at all times for every read at every site node
All reads always return the latest written value at every site instantly
All replicas will converge to the same value if no new updates are made
explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees that replicas will converge to the same value eventually if updates stop.

text: The quorum-based protocol requires:
options: Qr = Qw = 1 for consistency meaning only one replica needs to respond
Read quorum (Qr) + Write quorum (Qw) > total replicas (N) for consistency
No quorum is needed and any single replica can respond to operations
Qr + Qw less than N for consistency between read and write quorums
explanation: Quorum protocols require Qr + Qw > N and 2*Qw > N to ensure read-write and write-write consistency.

text: Vector clocks in distributed systems are used to:
options: Capture causal relationships between events at different sites
Measure CPU speed and processing capacity at each network node
Optimize queries by selecting the best execution plan for joins
Tell wall-clock time accurately across all distributed node sites
explanation: Vector clocks track causality between events in a distributed system, determining potential concurrency.

text: Distributed hash tables (DHTs) are used in:
options: Peer-to-peer distributed storage systems
Centralized databases on a single machine
Single-machine databases without networks
Only for indexing within one database node
